Core Meaning: A development approach in which the requirements are subject to a high level of uncertainty and volatility and are likely to change throughout the project.
Expansion: An adaptive approach is used when the solution cannot be fully defined upfront and learning is expected during delivery. Work is planned progressively, with frequent feedback used to adjust scope, priorities, and design. PMI positions it as appropriate where uncertainty is high and responsiveness creates better outcomes than rigid upfront prediction.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An adaptive approach is a way of delivering work through short cycles, progressive elaboration, and continuous reprioritisation. It accepts that requirements will evolve and treats learning as part of delivery rather than as rework to be avoided.

Why it matters and where it applies: some projects operate in environments where customer needs, technology, or external conditions change too quickly for a fixed plan to remain valid. Adaptive approaches are especially useful in product development, software, innovation work, and other contexts with high uncertainty.

The most common misconception: that adaptive means unstructured or uncontrolled. In practice, it still relies on governance, planning, and accountability, but these are applied iteratively and updated as evidence emerges.

How it shows up in real projects: teams maintain a prioritised backlog, deliver in increments, gather stakeholder feedback frequently, and revise plans based on actual learning rather than only original assumptions.
Contrast: Predictive approach — an adaptive approach expects requirements to evolve and plans progressively; a predictive approach seeks to define scope and plans more fully upfront.
Example: A digital-services team releases a minimum viable feature set, gathers user analytics and stakeholder feedback, and updates the backlog each iteration as needs become clearer.
Source: Agile Practice Guide — Adaptive approach

Core Meaning: A life cycle in which scope is elaborated over time and work is delivered through multiple iterations or increments in response to change.
Expansion: An adaptive life cycle is used when requirements are uncertain, likely to evolve, or best understood through feedback during delivery. Rather than defining all scope in detail at the start, the team progressively refines plans and delivers value in small increments or iterations. PMI positions it as a life cycle suited to high-change environments where learning, stakeholder input, and rapid reprioritisation improve outcomes. It is commonly associated with agile ways of working, but the core idea is the progressive adaptation of scope, plans, and delivery as more is learned.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An adaptive life cycle is a project life cycle designed for environments where uncertainty is high and requirements cannot be fully defined upfront. Work is planned and executed in short cycles, with learning from each cycle shaping what comes next.

Why it matters and where it applies: It helps teams respond to changing customer needs, emerging technical understanding, and shifting priorities without treating change as failure. It is especially relevant in software, product development, innovation, and other domains where early feedback improves the final result.

The most common misconception: that adaptive means unstructured or unmanaged. In practice, adaptive projects still use planning, governance, prioritisation, and controls, but they apply them progressively and iteratively rather than trying to lock down all details at the beginning.

How it shows up in real projects: Teams maintain a prioritised backlog, deliver usable increments frequently, review outcomes with stakeholders, and revise upcoming work based on what was learned in prior iterations.
Contrast: Predictive life cycle — an adaptive life cycle expects scope to evolve through iteration, while a predictive life cycle defines scope as fully as possible early and manages to the baseline.
Example: A digital-service project releases a basic online application feature first, gathers user feedback, and then reprioritises later iterations to improve the highest-value functions.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / PMI Lexicon — confirm exact citation in Infinity

Core Meaning: Planning that proceeds in short, iterative cycles, progressively refining detail as requirements and conditions become clearer.
Expansion: Adaptive planning replaces a single fixed plan with frequent replanning as the team learns from delivered increments and feedback. Near-term work is planned in detail while longer-term work is held at a coarse level until needed. This embraces change and reduces the cost of decisions made under uncertainty.
Contrast: Differs from predictive planning, which fixes scope and schedule up front; adaptive planning keeps the plan deliberately responsive to change.
Example: The team plans only the next two iterations in detail and revises the roadmap after each customer demo.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: A mindset of values and principles, set out in the Manifesto for Agile Software Development, for delivering value under conditions of change.
Expansion: Agile prioritises customer collaboration, frequent delivery, and responsiveness to change over rigid adherence to an upfront plan. PMI treats it as a values-based way of working rather than a fixed set of ceremonies, expressed through methods such as Scrum and Kanban but not reducible to them. It is most useful where requirements are uncertain and rapid feedback improves outcomes.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Agile is a mindset built on iterative delivery, collaboration, and continuous learning rather than a single method. Teams plan progressively, deliver in small increments, and adjust as they learn.

Why it matters and where it applies: many projects face shifting customer needs and uncertain solutions. Agile shortens feedback loops and surfaces risk early, which is valuable in software, product development, and other change-heavy work.

The most common misconception: that Agile means no planning, documentation, or governance. In practice it keeps all three, but applies them in short cycles that serve learning and value rather than process for its own sake.

How it shows up in real projects: prioritised backlogs, iteration cadences, review demos, retrospectives, and release decisions driven by user feedback and measurable outcomes.
Contrast: Adaptive approach — Agile is the underlying mindset of values and principles; an adaptive approach is the broader delivery approach used in changing environments.
Example: A mobile-app team ships a small set of user stories every two weeks, demos the increment to stakeholders, and reprioritises based on customer feedback.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, Section 2 and glossary, pages 8-10 and glossary page citation to be confirmed against edition in use

Core Meaning: The statement of values and principles that underpins Agile ways of working.
Expansion: The Agile Manifesto expresses the foundational values and principles behind Agile rather than prescribing a single method. PMI uses it to explain why Agile emphasises collaboration, responsiveness to change, working outcomes, and continuous learning. It matters because many Agile frameworks and practices make sense only when interpreted through those underlying values.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The Agile Manifesto is the short foundational statement created in 2001 that defines the core values and principles of Agile. It is not a delivery framework, governance model, or lifecycle by itself.

Why it matters and where it applies: It gives teams and leaders a common basis for choosing practices that support adaptability and value delivery. It is especially relevant in environments with evolving requirements, frequent feedback, and uncertainty about the best solution path.

The most common misconception: that the manifesto tells teams to reject planning, documentation, or discipline. In practice it reframes them so they support customer value and learning instead of becoming ends in themselves.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use it to justify incremental delivery, backlog reprioritisation, stakeholder collaboration, and regular retrospectives when conditions change or new information appears.
Contrast: Agile Practice Guide — the Agile Manifesto states the values and principles; the Agile Practice Guide explains how those ideas can be applied in delivery.
Example: A product team decides to shorten release cycles and involve users in each review because the manifesto's values push them toward faster feedback and closer collaboration.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, Appendix X3, Manifesto for Agile Software Development, page 167

Core Meaning: A PMI practice guide that explains agile principles and provides practical guidance for using agile and hybrid approaches on projects.
Expansion: The Agile Practice Guide was developed to help practitioners understand when and how to apply agile, iterative, incremental, and hybrid ways of working. It complements PMI standards by translating agile values and principles into practical guidance on team structures, delivery life cycles, stakeholder engagement, and organizational considerations. It is commonly used as a reference for tailoring project approaches in environments with uncertainty, evolving requirements, or rapid feedback needs.
Contrast: PMBOK Guide — the PMBOK Guide provides a broader project management standard and body of knowledge, while the Agile Practice Guide focuses specifically on agile and hybrid application.
Example: A product-development team uses the Agile Practice Guide to decide how to combine Scrum-based delivery with stage-gate governance required by its organization.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, page 1

Core Meaning: An iterative, adaptive approach to delivering projects through short cycles of work and frequent feedback.
Expansion: Agile project management delivers value incrementally through short iterations rather than a single long sequence of phases, emphasizing collaboration, customer feedback, and responsiveness to change. It is grounded in the values and principles of the Agile Manifesto and is well suited to projects with high uncertainty or evolving requirements. Common frameworks include Scrum, Kanban, and XP, and progress is measured by working deliverables rather than documentation alone.
Contrast: Predictive (waterfall) project management - predictive approaches plan scope and schedule in detail up front, whereas agile embraces change and refines the plan iteration by iteration.
Example: A software team delivering working features in two-week sprints, reprioritizing the backlog after each demo with the customer.
Source: Agile Practice Guide - agile approaches

Core Meaning: A cross-functional group of people who collaborate to deliver value in small increments using agile ways of working.
Expansion: An agile team is typically structured to contain the skills needed to complete work with minimal handoffs and fast feedback. PMI describes agile teams as collaborative, self-managing, and focused on delivering customer value through frequent increments. The concept matters because agile delivery depends not just on iterative planning, but on a team design that supports adaptation, transparency, and continuous improvement.
Contrast: Project team — a project team is the broader group assigned to project work, while an agile team is specifically organized for iterative, collaborative, value-focused delivery.
Example: A digital-service team includes a product owner, developers, tester, UX designer, and analyst who work together each iteration to release usable features.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, page 41

Core Meaning: The ongoing ordering of backlog items so the most valuable work is done first.
Expansion: Backlog prioritisation arranges the product backlog so that items delivering the greatest value, or reducing the most risk, sit at the top ready to be pulled into the next iteration. The product owner continually re-ranks items as value, cost, dependencies, and new information change. Techniques such as MoSCoW, weighted shortest job first, and cost of delay inform the ordering.
Contrast: Backlog refinement clarifies and sizes items, while prioritisation decides their relative order.
Example: A product owner moves a high-value compliance feature to the top of the backlog ahead of cosmetic improvements.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: The ongoing activity of reviewing, clarifying, splitting, estimating, and reprioritising backlog items so they are ready for future planning and delivery.
Expansion: Backlog refinement keeps upcoming work sufficiently understood before the team commits to it. It typically includes clarifying scope, adding acceptance details, breaking large items into smaller ones, and adjusting order based on value and feedback. This practice improves flow and reduces planning friction in agile and adaptive environments. PMI recognises it as a common practitioner activity in backlog-driven delivery, not as a formal core glossary term.
Contrast: Sprint planning — backlog refinement prepares items ahead of time, while sprint planning selects and commits near-term work for the iteration.
Example: Before the next iteration, a product team reviews the top backlog items, adds acceptance criteria, and splits a large feature into several smaller user stories.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The quantity of work moved through a process or released together at one time.
Expansion: Batch size is the amount of work or number of items processed as a single unit before being passed downstream. Lean and agile thinking favor small batch sizes because they shorten feedback cycles, reduce work in progress, and surface problems sooner. Large batches delay feedback and amplify the cost of defects.
Contrast: Unlike a one-piece flow that moves single items continuously, large batch sizes accumulate work before handing it off, increasing cycle time.
Example: Releasing software in small weekly increments rather than a yearly mega-release reduces batch size and risk.
Source: Lean and agile practice; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An approach that begins with understanding how the user will use a product, writes tests for that behavior, and then constructs solutions against the tests.
Expansion: Behavior-driven development focuses on expected behavior from the user's perspective. It expresses requirements in a form that is easier for business and technical stakeholders to share. PMI's business analysis material describes it as starting with user behavior, then writing tests for that behavior, and then building the solution to satisfy those tests.
Contrast: Test-driven development — BDD frames behavior in user-readable terms; TDD focuses more directly on tests that drive implementation.
Example: A team defines login behavior in terms of user-visible outcomes, writes tests for those outcomes, and then builds the authentication feature.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, Behavior-driven development (BDD), page 389

Core Meaning: A condition in which an activity, task, or work item cannot proceed because a dependency, constraint, or impediment is preventing progress.
Expansion: Blocked is commonly used in project and agile contexts to indicate that work is waiting on something external or unresolved. It signals that the team needs help removing the obstacle before progress can continue. In practice, blocked items are often tracked so that impediments are visible and can be escalated quickly.
Contrast: Impediment — blocked describes the current state of work that cannot proceed; an impediment is the underlying obstacle causing that state.
Example: A developer's task is blocked because the security team has not yet approved access to the test environment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Events or conditions that halt work or prevent further progress.
Expansion: Blockers are obstacles that stop a team member or the team from making progress on their work until they are resolved. In agile practice they are surfaced quickly - often in the daily standup - so they can be removed promptly, frequently by the Scrum Master or team lead.
Contrast: Impediment - the terms are often used interchangeably, but 'blocker' usually denotes a hard stop on a specific work item, whereas 'impediment' is any broader hindrance to team progress.
Example: A developer cannot continue coding because the test environment is down - a blocker raised at the daily standup.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A limiting point in a workflow, resource system, or process that constrains overall throughput.
Expansion: A bottleneck is the step, role, or constraint that restricts how fast work can move through the system as a whole. In project and delivery settings, it is used to diagnose delays, queue buildup, handoff friction, and uneven flow rather than simply to label any problem area. Managing a bottleneck improves overall system performance more effectively than optimising unconstrained parts of the work.
Contrast: Blocker — a bottleneck constrains flow for multiple items or the system overall, while a blocker stops a specific task or item from progressing until the obstacle is removed.
Example: In a product release project, security review becomes the bottleneck because every feature waits in queue for the same small specialist team before deployment can proceed.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A visual chart that shows work completed or work remaining over time so a team can track progress against an iteration, release, or project plan.
Expansion: A burn chart is a lightweight visual control used in adaptive delivery to show whether work is being completed at the expected pace. It commonly appears as a burndown chart, which tracks remaining work, or a burnup chart, which tracks completed work against total scope. Teams use it to inspect progress quickly, spot delivery risk early, and support forecasting conversations.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A burn chart plots work against time to make delivery progress visible. Depending on the chart type, it shows either how much work remains or how much has been completed.

Why it matters and where it applies: teams need a simple way to see whether they are on track within a sprint, release, or other short planning horizon. Burn charts support transparency, daily coordination, and evidence-based conversations about pace, scope, and predictability in agile and hybrid environments.

The most common misconception: that a burn chart is a performance score for individuals or a guarantee of delivery. In practice it is a team-level information radiator, and its usefulness depends on stable definitions of work, timely updates, and thoughtful interpretation.

How it shows up in real projects: a Scrum team reviews a sprint burndown during the daily stand-up, notices that remaining effort is not falling as expected, and then re-plans tasks, removes blockers, or renegotiates scope.
Contrast: Velocity — a burn chart shows progress over time within a timebox or release, while velocity measures the amount of work a team typically completes per iteration.
Example: A software team tracks remaining story points each day of a two-week sprint on a burndown chart to see whether the sprint goal is still achievable.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, burnup and burndown chart entries in glossary section

Core Meaning: The rate at which an agile team consumes effort or budget relative to the work remaining.
Expansion: Burn rate measures how quickly a team is using up its available hours or budget to complete work. In agile contexts it often refers to the pace at which the team is consuming the effort set aside for the iteration or release, and it can be read from burndown data. A burn rate higher than planned warns that capacity or budget will be exhausted before the work is done.
Contrast: Velocity - velocity measures how much work the team completes per iteration, whereas burn rate focuses on how fast effort or money is being consumed.
Example: A team that has spent 60% of its sprint hours with only 40% of stories done has a burn rate that signals trouble.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of the work remaining versus the time left in an iteration, release, or project.
Expansion: A burndown chart visualizes how much work remains over time so a team can quickly see whether progress is tracking toward completion. It is commonly used in adaptive delivery settings such as iterations or releases to support transparency and short-cycle control. The chart is useful because it highlights pace, trend, and potential slippage without requiring a complex status narrative.
Contrast: Burnup chart — a burndown chart shows remaining work decreasing over time; a burnup chart shows completed work increasing and can also make scope growth more visible.
Example: A Scrum team tracks remaining story points each day of a two-week sprint on a burndown chart to see whether the sprint goal is still achievable.

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of work completed over time compared with the total scope.
Expansion: A burnup chart shows cumulative completed work rising over time against a line representing total scope. PMI highlights it as a simple visual tool for tracking progress in adaptive environments because it makes both delivery and scope change easier to see. This helps teams and stakeholders distinguish slow progress from expanding scope, which can be harder to interpret on a burndown chart alone.
Contrast: Burndown chart — a burnup chart emphasizes completed work and visibility of scope growth; a burndown chart emphasizes remaining work declining over time.
Example: A product team uses a burnup chart during a release to show completed features increasing while also revealing that added scope is pushing the target line upward.
