Core Meaning: Cadence is the regular rhythm or frequency at which project activities, events, or deliveries occur.
Expansion: Cadence gives work a predictable timing pattern, such as a two-week review cycle, a monthly steering meeting, or a fixed release rhythm. In PMI and agile usage, it supports coordination, inspection, planning, and stakeholder alignment by making key activities recur consistently. It is especially relevant in adaptive and iterative delivery, though it can also apply in predictive governance and reporting structures.
Contrast: Iteration — cadence is the repeating rhythm of work or events; an iteration is one time-boxed cycle that occurs within that rhythm.
Example: A delivery team uses a two-week cadence for planning, review, and retrospective meetings so stakeholders know exactly when to give feedback.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Cadence, page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: A Scrum metaphor distinguishing those committed to the work (pigs) from those merely involved (chickens).
Expansion: Drawn from a fable about a pig and chicken opening a restaurant, the metaphor labels people committed and accountable for delivering the sprint as pigs, and interested but not accountable stakeholders as chickens. Historically it governed who could speak in the daily scrum, though modern Scrum has largely retired the terminology as potentially demeaning.
Contrast: Stakeholder - all chickens and pigs are stakeholders; the metaphor specifically separates committed delivery team members from merely involved observers.
Example: During the daily standup the Scrum Master reminds attending managers they are chickens and should observe rather than redirect the team.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A practice in which developers examine each other's source code to find defects and improve quality.
Expansion: Code review is the systematic inspection of code changes by one or more peers before they are merged, aimed at catching defects, improving design, and sharing knowledge across the team. It can be synchronous, as in pair programming, or asynchronous through pull-request comments, and it reinforces shared standards and collective ownership of the codebase. Beyond finding bugs, it spreads understanding and reduces single points of knowledge.
Contrast: Testing executes the code to detect failures, while code review reads the code to assess quality and correctness.
Example: A developer opens a pull request and a teammate comments on edge cases before approving the merge.
Source: Software engineering practice; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A practice where the whole team shares responsibility for the work product rather than individuals owning parts.
Expansion: Collective ownership means any team member can improve any part of the shared work, and the team as a whole is accountable for its quality. Originating in Extreme Programming as collective code ownership, it reduces bottlenecks and single points of failure and spreads knowledge across the team. It depends on shared standards, trust, and practices such as pairing to keep quality consistent.
Contrast: Self-organizing team — collective ownership is shared responsibility for the work product, while a self-organizing team is one that decides for itself how to do the work.
Example: Any developer on the team can refactor a module they did not write because the codebase is collectively owned.
Source: Extreme Programming (Kent Beck) — collective code ownership

Core Meaning: The practice of making feature increments ready for immediate customer delivery through small batches of work and extensive automation.
Expansion: PMI defines continuous delivery as the practice of delivering feature increments immediately to customers, often through small batches of work and automation technology. It supports fast, reliable flow from completed work to usable value by reducing manual handoffs and release friction. The concept is especially important in adaptive and product-focused environments where rapid feedback improves outcomes and lowers delivery risk.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Continuous delivery is a way of working in which teams design, build, test, and prepare increments so they can be released at any time with minimal delay. It depends on small batch sizes, automated testing, stable integration practices, and release readiness built into everyday work.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because long delays between development and release hide defects, increase coordination cost, and slow learning. It is especially useful in software, digital products, platform services, and other environments where frequent deployment and customer feedback improve value delivery.

The most common misconception: that continuous delivery means every change must be released instantly to production. In practice, it means the increment is kept in a releasable state at all times, while the business may still choose when to release.

How it shows up in real projects: Teams automate builds and tests, integrate code frequently, use deployment pipelines, and release approved increments in small, low-risk updates instead of bundling large releases.
Contrast: Continuous integration — continuous delivery keeps increments ready for immediate release; continuous integration focuses on frequently merging and validating work in a shared codebase.
Example: A digital-banking team automates build, test, security scan, and deployment steps so a completed account-alert feature can be released safely as soon as product approval is given.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, p. 16

Core Meaning: A development practice in which team members frequently merge work into a shared codebase and validate it through automated checks.
Expansion: Continuous integration is a technical practice used in agile and product-development environments to integrate small changes into a common baseline on a frequent basis. Each integration is typically verified with automated builds and tests so defects surface early rather than accumulating until later. In project terms, it supports rapid feedback, lowers integration risk, and improves delivery flow in iterative work.
Contrast: Continuous delivery — continuous integration focuses on frequently merging and validating changes; continuous delivery extends that flow toward release readiness.
Example: A software team merges code changes several times a day and runs automated unit and integration tests after each commit to detect defects immediately.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The observation that a system's design tends to mirror the communication structure of the organisation that builds it.
Expansion: Melvin Conway observed that any organisation designing a system will produce a design whose structure copies the organisation's own communication patterns. In practice, teams that communicate poorly tend to build poorly integrated interfaces, while clean, modular architectures emerge from well-bounded, well-communicating teams. This insight motivates the inverse Conway manoeuvre, deliberately shaping team structure to encourage the desired architecture.
Contrast: Inverse Conway manoeuvre - Conway's law describes how organisation shapes system design, whereas the inverse manoeuvre deliberately restructures teams to drive a target architecture.
Example: A program splits one large component team into service-aligned squads so the software naturally evolves into cleanly separated services.
Source: Conway - How Do Committees Invent? (Conway's law)

Core Meaning: The economic impact of postponing the delivery of value, expressed as value lost per unit of time.
Expansion: Cost of delay quantifies how much value is forgone for each period a feature or project is late, combining the value at stake with its urgency over time. It makes the economics of timing explicit so teams can prioritize work that loses the most value if delayed. Frameworks such as weighted shortest job first use cost of delay divided by job size to sequence a backlog.
Contrast: A cost overrun is spending above budget, while cost of delay is value lost from late delivery regardless of spend.
Example: A team prioritizes a feature whose market window closes soon because its cost of delay is highest.
Source: Scaled Agile Framework

Core Meaning: A visual chart that shows how work items accumulate across workflow states over time to help monitor flow and bottlenecks.
Expansion: A cumulative flow diagram displays the quantity of work in each stage of a process, such as requested, in progress, and done, across a timeline. It helps teams assess throughput, work in progress, and stability of flow by making queues and delays visible. In agile and Kanban-oriented project work, it is used to spot bottlenecks, manage capacity, and support data-based improvement of delivery performance.
Contrast: Burndown chart — a cumulative flow diagram shows work distribution across states over time, while a burndown chart focuses on remaining work against time.
Example: A Kanban team tracks how many features sit in analysis, development, testing, and done each week and notices the testing band widening, revealing a persistent bottleneck.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The total elapsed time from when work starts on an item until that item is completed.
Expansion: In PMI usage, cycle time measures how long a work item takes once active work begins and ends when the item is finished. It is commonly used in adaptive and flow-based environments to evaluate delivery speed, reveal bottlenecks, and improve predictability. Teams often track it across similar work items to understand system performance rather than to judge individual effort.
Contrast: Lead time — cycle time measures elapsed time after work starts, while lead time usually begins earlier from request or commitment.
Example: A software team starts implementing a defect fix on Monday and releases it on Thursday, so the defect's cycle time is four days.
Source: PMI Lexicon / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, term Cycle Time

Core Meaning: A visual chart used in flow-based or iterative work to show how long work items take to move through the process so teams can inspect throughput and delivery speed.
Expansion: A cycle time chart displays the elapsed time for work items from the moment work starts until the item is finished. In PMI-aligned agile and Kanban contexts, it helps teams see delivery patterns, identify delay, and monitor whether work is flowing predictably. It is primarily a practical visualisation built from the underlying concept of cycle time rather than a formal PMI glossary term.
Contrast: Burndown chart — a cycle time chart tracks elapsed time per work item through a workflow, while a burndown chart tracks remaining work against time.
Example: A Kanban team plots the cycle time of each service request over a month and sees that items involving legal review take much longer than others, prompting a workflow change.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A short recurring team meeting used to synchronise work, surface impediments, and coordinate near-term action.
Expansion: A daily coordination meeting is a brief cadence-based touchpoint in which team members align on progress, immediate priorities, and blockers. It supports transparency and rapid adjustment, especially in iterative and flow-based environments. In PMI-related usage it is a practical team-coordination term, often overlapping with agile daily meetings, rather than a distinct formal glossary entry.
Contrast: Daily Scrum — a daily coordination meeting is the broader coordination practice, while the Daily Scrum is the specific Scrum event with a defined purpose inside the Scrum framework.
Example: Each morning, a cross-functional delivery team spends 15 minutes reviewing what changed since yesterday, what is planned today, and which dependencies need immediate resolution.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A short, time-boxed daily event where the development team synchronizes work and plans the next day toward the sprint goal.
Expansion: The Daily Scrum is held at the same time and place each day and is limited to about fifteen minutes. The team inspects progress toward the sprint goal and adapts its plan, surfacing impediments for later resolution. It is for the developers, keeping coordination self-organized rather than a status report to managers.
Contrast: Differs from a sprint review, which inspects the product increment with stakeholders; the Daily Scrum inspects daily progress within the team.
Example: Each morning the developers spend fifteen minutes agreeing what to tackle next and flagging a blocked integration task.
Source: The Scrum Guide

Core Meaning: A brief daily collaboration meeting in which the team reviews recent progress, states near-term intentions, and raises obstacles.
Expansion: In PMI terminology, this is aligned with the daily coordination meeting and reflects the common practice formerly called a standup meeting. Its purpose is to synchronise team members quickly, expose impediments early, and support short-cycle delivery. It is most common in agile and hybrid environments where teams coordinate work frequently.
Contrast: Status meeting — a daily standup is a short team coordination event focused on immediate work and impediments, not a broad reporting session for management.
Example: Each morning, a delivery team spends fifteen minutes sharing what was completed yesterday, what will be worked on today, and what blockers need attention.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Daily coordination meeting, p. 7

Core Meaning: A formal shared description of the state a product increment must meet to be considered complete.
Expansion: Definition of Done sets the quality and completion conditions that an increment, feature, or work item must satisfy before the team treats it as finished. It creates a common understanding across the team and stakeholders about what complete means, reducing ambiguity and hidden rework. In agile delivery, it often includes coding, testing, integration, documentation, compliance, and approval expectations.
Deep Dive:
Definition of Done is a working agreement that establishes the minimum conditions an increment must meet before it counts as complete. It gives teams a consistent completion standard so that items counted as done are genuinely usable and not partially finished. Depending on the team, those conditions commonly include:

- Coding complete
- Testing performed
- Integration done
- Documentation updated
- Compliance requirements met
- Defect thresholds satisfied
- Required approvals obtained

Why it matters and where it applies: it improves transparency, predictability, and quality in iterative delivery. It is especially important in agile and hybrid environments where teams produce frequent increments and need a reliable basis for review, release, and progress measurement.

The most common misconception is that done simply means development work is finished. In practice, done usually includes broader expectations such as testing, integration, documentation, defect thresholds, and any other conditions needed for the increment to be considered complete.

How it shows up in real projects: teams post the Definition of Done visibly, use it during iteration planning and reviews, and check every backlog item against it before accepting it into an increment or release.
Contrast: Acceptance criteria — Definition of Done sets the common completion standard for all items or increments; acceptance criteria define the specific conditions for an individual requirement or backlog item.
Example: A Scrum team defines done as coded, peer reviewed, unit tested, integrated, security checked, documented, and accepted by the product owner before a story can count toward the sprint increment.
Source: Agile Practice Guide — Definition of Done

Core Meaning: A team's checklist for a user-centric requirement that has all the information the team needs to begin working on it.
Expansion: Definition of Ready is a working agreement that helps a team decide whether a backlog item is sufficiently understood and prepared for implementation. It improves flow by reducing ambiguity at the point work starts. Teams often use it in adaptive delivery to confirm that acceptance criteria, dependencies, and needed information are in place.
Contrast: Definition of Done — Definition of Ready sets the entry criteria for starting work; Definition of Done sets the completion criteria for finishing it.
Example: Before pulling a user story into an iteration, a product team checks that the story has acceptance criteria, dependencies identified, and stakeholder clarifications resolved.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Definition of Ready, p. 7

Core Meaning: The Scrum Team members who commit to creating any aspect of a usable increment each Sprint.
Expansion: Developers are the people on the Scrum Team accountable for creating a plan for the Sprint, instilling quality, adapting their plan daily, and holding each other accountable as professionals. The term covers anyone doing the work of building the product, not only programmers. Introduced in the 2020 Scrum Guide, it replaced Development Team to emphasize one unified team.
Contrast: Developers differ from the Product Owner and Scrum Master, who hold distinct accountabilities for value and process within the same Scrum Team.
Example: On a marketing product, the Developers include a copywriter, a designer, and an analyst who together produce the Sprint increment.
Source: Scrum Guide

Core Meaning: In earlier Scrum, the cross-functional group responsible for delivering a usable increment each Sprint.
Expansion: The Development Team was the self-organizing, cross-functional set of professionals who did the work of building the increment in Scrum. It had no sub-titles or internal hierarchy and was accountable as a whole for the output of each Sprint. The 2020 Scrum Guide replaced the term with Developers, folding them into a single Scrum Team to remove the sense of a team within a team.
Contrast: Unlike a traditional project team led by a manager who assigns tasks, the Development Team self-organized to decide how to turn Backlog items into an increment.
Example: A Scrum Development Team of five engineers and a designer collectively committed to delivering the Sprint Backlog.
Source: Scrum Guide

Core Meaning: A collaborative way of working that integrates software development and operations to enable faster, more reliable delivery and support of products and services.
Expansion: DevOps brings development and operations practices together so teams can build, test, release, and run digital products with tighter feedback loops. In PMI-aligned practice, it is commonly associated with automation, continuous integration, continuous delivery, and shared accountability for outcomes. It is especially relevant in digital delivery environments where speed, quality, and operational stability must be balanced.
Contrast: Agile — Agile is a broader mindset for adaptive value delivery; DevOps focuses specifically on integrating build-and-run practices across development and operations.
Example: A software team automates code integration, testing, deployment, and production monitoring so new features can be released weekly with fewer operational incidents.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A process decision toolkit that helps teams choose ways of working fit for their context.
Expansion: Disciplined Agile is PMI's toolkit for tailoring practices, lifecycle choices, and operating models to the needs of a team or organisation. Rather than prescribing one method, it guides people to select context-appropriate ways of working across agile, predictive, and hybrid environments. It is especially useful where teams need flexibility with governance, architecture, value delivery, and enterprise alignment.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Disciplined Agile is a toolkit and mindset for choosing how work gets done based on the situation rather than by following a single predefined method. It offers guidance across team, value-stream, and enterprise levels.

Why it matters and where it applies: many organisations operate in mixed environments where one method does not fit every initiative. Disciplined Agile helps teams tailor practices while still supporting coordination, governance, and business outcomes.

The most common misconception: that Disciplined Agile is just another agile framework competing directly with Scrum. In practice it is broader and helps teams decide when Scrum, Kanban, hybrid planning, lean governance, or other options make sense.

How it shows up in real projects: a team uses Disciplined Agile guidance to choose an iteration-based lifecycle, lightweight architecture reviews, and context-specific governance checkpoints instead of adopting a standard method unchanged.
Contrast: Scrum — Disciplined Agile is a decision toolkit for choosing fit-for-purpose ways of working; Scrum is a specific framework with defined roles and events.
Example: A PMO supports software, infrastructure, and compliance projects by using Disciplined Agile guidance so each team adopts a different lifecycle and governance approach suited to its delivery context.
Source: Disciplined Agile, Glossary, Disciplined Agile, p. 486

Core Meaning: A version of a product increment that is made available for review, testing, or use.
Expansion: Drop refers to a released build or version of a solution component, often used in iterative and incremental delivery contexts. It helps teams expose work for feedback, validation, integration, or deployment in controlled steps.
Contrast: Increment — a drop is a specific released version made available at a point in time; an increment is the usable addition of functionality or capability itself.
Example: A software team provides a monthly test drop of the application so business users can validate new features before the production release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Managing work through transparency, inspection, and adaptation based on observation rather than detailed up-front planning.
Expansion: Empirical process control is the foundation of Scrum and other agile approaches. It rests on three pillars - transparency, inspection, and adaptation - and holds that knowledge comes from experience and decisions are based on what is actually observed. It suits work with high uncertainty, where a fully predictive plan would quickly become wrong.
Contrast: Defined process control - defined control assumes a repeatable, well-understood process can be planned in detail up front, whereas empirical control inspects and adapts as the work reveals new information.
Example: A Scrum team inspects the product increment at each sprint review and adapts the backlog, rather than following a fixed multi-year plan.
Source: Scrum framework - empirical process control (Agile Practice Guide)

Core Meaning: The principle that knowledge comes from experience and that decisions should be based on what is observed.
Expansion: Empiricism is the idea that the best way to plan is to do the work and learn from it, rather than to rely on extensive prediction. It is a foundational belief behind Scrum and agile approaches, where teams build something small, observe the results, and use that learning to decide what to do next. It values evidence over assumption in conditions of uncertainty.
Contrast: Empirical process control - empiricism is the underlying idea that we learn by doing, whereas empirical process control is the operational framework (transparency, inspection, adaptation) that puts it into practice.
Example: Rather than locking down a fixed scope, an agile team ships a small slice, learns from real user feedback, and adjusts the next iteration.
Source: Agile Practice Guide - empiricism in agile

Core Meaning: A large, related body of work intended to hierarchically organize a set of requirements and deliver specific business outcomes.
Expansion: An epic groups related requirements into a larger item that can be broken down into smaller pieces for planning and delivery. It is commonly used in adaptive and hybrid environments to organize work at a higher level before decomposition. PMI describes it as a way to structure requirements around business outcomes.
Contrast: Feature — an epic is a larger body of related work that can be decomposed into features or smaller requirements; a feature is a smaller, more specific capability.
Example: A digital banking program creates an epic for mobile account opening, then breaks it into smaller features such as identity verification, document upload, and approval workflow.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase / Agile Practice Guide — epic

Core Meaning: The disciplined practice of running small, controlled tests to validate assumptions and reduce uncertainty before committing to a full solution.
Expansion: In adaptive and lean delivery, experimentation treats uncertain features or strategies as testable propositions checked cheaply against real users or data. Teams design minimal tests, measure outcomes, and use the evidence to decide whether to persevere or change course. This lowers the cost of being wrong and accelerates learning where ambiguity is high. Experimentation underpins practices such as split testing and the minimum viable product.
Contrast: Big design up front — up-front design commits to a complete solution before building, whereas experimentation defers commitment until evidence accumulates.
Example: A product team releases two pricing pages to a small user segment to learn which converts better before a full rollout.
Source: Agile Practice Guide (PMI and Agile Alliance)

Core Meaning: An agile software development method emphasizing technical excellence, frequent feedback, and close customer collaboration.
Expansion: Extreme Programming, or XP, is an agile approach built on short development cycles and disciplined engineering practices that keep software flexible and high in quality. Its practices include pair programming, test-driven development, continuous integration, simple design, refactoring, and an on-site customer. XP aims to lower the cost of change so that requirements can evolve throughout delivery without degrading the codebase.
Contrast: Scrum focuses on team workflow and roles, while Extreme Programming prescribes specific engineering practices for how the code is built.
Example: A development team adopts XP, pairing on code and writing automated tests before each feature to sustain quality.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: An agile practice of running small, frequent experiments to surface failures early and learn from them quickly.
Expansion: Fail fast is an agile mindset of testing ideas in small, low-cost increments so that flawed approaches are discovered and discarded early, before large investment. The aim is rapid learning and course correction, not failure as a goal in itself.
Contrast: Big-bang delivery - big-bang delivery commits to a full solution before validating it, whereas fail fast validates incrementally to catch problems cheaply.
Example: A team builds a throwaway prototype in two days to test a risky assumption before committing months of development.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The number of items passing through a process.
Expansion: Flow is a simple measure of how much work moves through a system. It is useful for understanding delivery performance and the health of a process over time.
Contrast: Cycle time — flow measures how many items move through the process, while cycle time measures how long an individual item takes to move through it.
Example: A service desk tracks how many support tickets move from intake to resolution each week to understand whether work is moving smoothly.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase, term Flow

Core Meaning: Measures that show how work moves through a delivery system and how effectively value is being completed.
Expansion: Flow metrics are used in agile and flow-based environments to understand the rate, stability, and efficiency of work delivery. Common examples include cycle time, throughput, work in progress, and flow efficiency. They help teams identify bottlenecks, improve predictability, and make data-informed process adjustments.
Contrast: Earned value metrics — flow metrics examine movement of work through a system, while earned value metrics compare planned and actual performance against scope, schedule, and cost baselines.
Example: A product team tracks cycle time and work in progress on its Kanban board to reduce delays in releasing customer features.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A team member with deep expertise in one area and enough breadth to contribute across multiple areas of work.
Expansion: A generalizing specialist combines specialist depth with broader working knowledge so they can collaborate across disciplines and adapt to changing project needs. PMI uses the term in agile and hybrid contexts to describe people who are not narrowly siloed. This capability helps teams reduce handoffs, improve flow, and respond more flexibly to uncertainty.
Contrast: Specialist — a generalizing specialist has both depth and breadth; a specialist is primarily deep in one domain.
Example: A developer on a digital product team writes code, helps clarify user stories, and supports test design during iteration planning.
Source: Agile Practice Guide / Generalizing Specialists, p. 42

Core Meaning: A hardening sprint is a timebox used to stabilize, integrate, fix defects, and prepare a product increment for release.
Expansion: Teams use this term to describe a dedicated period for activities such as integration testing, performance tuning, defect remediation, and release readiness. It commonly appears in agile implementations that have not yet fully embedded quality practices into every iteration. PMI materials generally treat it as a practitioner usage rather than a core glossary concept, and many agile practitioners view heavy reliance on it as a sign that quality is being deferred too late.
Contrast: Definition of Done — a hardening sprint concentrates stabilization work in a later timebox, while Definition of Done aims to build release-quality work into every increment.
Example: A software team completes feature development over several iterations and then runs a hardening sprint to resolve integration defects before deployment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A hybrid approach is a combination of predictive, iterative, incremental, and or agile elements tailored to the project context.
Expansion: A hybrid approach blends practices from more than one delivery approach instead of applying a single method end to end. PMI presents it as a practical response to environments where some parts of the work are stable and others are uncertain or fast changing. The combination is intentional and tailored, not an arbitrary mixture of techniques. Its value comes from matching governance, planning, and delivery mechanics to the nature of the work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A hybrid approach combines methods and controls from different delivery styles within one project or product effort. For example, a team might use predictive planning for infrastructure and adaptive delivery for customer-facing features.

Why it matters and where it applies: many projects do not fit neatly into a single approach because requirements, risk levels, compliance demands, and stakeholder expectations vary across components. Hybrid working helps organisations preserve needed control while still gaining responsiveness where uncertainty is high.

The most common misconception: that hybrid simply means Agile with a few traditional documents added. In practice, a true hybrid approach is deliberately designed around the characteristics of the work, with clear reasoning for which elements are predictive and which are adaptive.

How it shows up in real projects: teams may maintain stage-gate governance and long-range milestone tracking while delivering solution components in short iterations, using backlog refinement and demos within an overall integrated master plan.
Contrast: Hybrid life cycle — a hybrid approach is the broader tailored way of working, while a hybrid life cycle is the time-phased delivery structure that mixes life cycle characteristics.
Example: An ERP programme uses predictive planning for data migration and regulatory approvals, while configuration and user-facing reports are developed iteratively with regular stakeholder reviews.
Source: PMI Lexicon / Agile Practice Guide, Hybrid approach

Core Meaning: A hybrid life cycle is a project life cycle that combines predictive and adaptive elements within the same overall delivery structure.
Expansion: A hybrid life cycle applies different life cycle characteristics to different parts of the work based on uncertainty, risk, and stakeholder needs. PMI uses it to describe situations where some deliverables can be planned in detail early while others benefit from iterative discovery. It enables organisations to align delivery cadence and control mechanisms with the nature of each component rather than forcing a single model across all work.
Contrast: Project life cycle — a project life cycle is the overall sequence of phases a project goes through, while a hybrid life cycle specifically mixes predictive and adaptive patterns within that sequence.
Example: A medical-device project manages hardware engineering through defined design phases but develops the companion software in short iterations with frequent user testing.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, Hybrid life cycle

Core Meaning: A testable assumption proposed as the basis for an experiment to learn whether it holds true.
Expansion: A hypothesis states a clear, falsifiable expectation, such as a belief that a feature will increase customer retention. In adaptive and lean approaches, teams frame work as hypotheses and run small experiments to validate or invalidate them before scaling. This reduces waste by replacing assumptions with evidence.
Contrast: Assumption — an assumption is taken as true without proof, while a hypothesis is explicitly framed to be tested and potentially disproved.
Example: A product team hypothesizes that one-click checkout will lift conversion by ten percent and runs an A/B test to confirm it.
Source: Lean Startup (Eric Ries) — hypothesis-driven experimentation

Core Meaning: Obstacles or conditions that block or slow an agile team's progress and need to be removed.
Expansion: Impediments are obstacles, conditions, or dependencies that hinder a team from progressing toward its goals, such as a pending decision, a broken build tool, or an unavailable stakeholder. In Scrum, surfacing and removing impediments is a core responsibility of the scrum master or team facilitator, often tracked on an impediment log. Quick removal protects the team's flow and throughput.
Contrast: Risk - a risk is an uncertain future event that may or may not occur, whereas an impediment is a present obstacle already blocking progress.
Example: A scrum master escalates a delayed test-environment setup that is preventing the team from completing its sprint.
Source: Agile Practice Guide - impediments

Core Meaning: The active elimination of obstacles that slow or block a team's ability to deliver value.
Expansion: Impediment removal is a common agile and servant leadership practice in which blockers are surfaced quickly and addressed so work can keep flowing. It is usually associated with team facilitation roles such as Scrum Master, delivery lead, or project leader, but responsibility can extend to anyone able to clear the path. PMI treats this as an applied practice within agile leadership and team performance rather than a core formal glossary term.
Contrast: Issue management — impediment removal focuses on clearing obstacles affecting team flow and execution, while issue management is the broader process for logging, analysing, and resolving project problems.
Example: A Scrum Master works with the infrastructure team to restore test-environment access that is preventing developers from completing stories in the current iteration.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A usable, tested, and potentially releasable addition to a product or deliverable created during iterative development.
Expansion: An increment is the concrete outcome of one or more iterations that adds measurable value to the evolving product or result. PMI uses the term in agile and adaptive delivery contexts to describe work that is integrated, verified, and ready for stakeholder review or release consideration. The idea is not merely that more work was done, but that the work was combined into something coherent and usable. This makes progress visible and supports frequent feedback, learning, and decision-making.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An increment is a completed slice of product functionality or deliverable content that is usable in its current state. It represents integrated work, not partially finished components waiting to be assembled later.

Why it matters and where it applies: increments make progress transparent and give teams a practical basis for feedback, acceptance, and release decisions. They are central in agile, hybrid, and iterative environments where value is delivered progressively rather than all at once at the end.

The most common misconception: that an increment is any amount of work completed during a timebox. In PMI usage, it is more than effort expended or tasks checked off; it is a working addition that can be evaluated as part of the whole.

How it shows up in real projects: a software team completes login, password reset, and audit tracking as an integrated capability that stakeholders can test, or a construction design team issues a coordinated package of approved drawings for one building zone.
Contrast: Iteration — an iteration is the timeboxed cycle of work; an increment is the usable outcome produced from that cycle or from several cycles.
Example: A product team finishes a search feature, integrates it with the existing application, passes testing, and demonstrates it to users as a working addition at the sprint review.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, term Increment, page 167

Core Meaning: A development or delivery pattern in which the product, service, or result is built and provided in successive portions that add usable capability.
Expansion: Incremental delivery produces the overall solution through a series of completed pieces rather than all at once. Each increment adds functionality to what has already been delivered and can provide value before the full scope is finished. PMI uses the term to distinguish staged build-out from a single end-of-project handoff.
Contrast: Iterative — incremental adds finished capability in pieces, while iterative refines or revisits the same work through repeated cycles.
Example: A campus-security project first deploys badge access for main entrances, then adds parking-gate control, then adds visitor-management capability as separate usable increments.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, page 170

Core Meaning: An adaptive development approach in which the deliverable is produced successively by adding functionality until it contains the necessary and sufficient capability to be considered complete.
Expansion: An incremental approach builds the final deliverable in pieces, with each piece adding usable functionality. PMI describes it as successive delivery of capability until the whole is complete. This helps teams provide value earlier, reduce exposure, and incorporate learning as the product evolves. It is especially useful when the overall solution is understood broadly but can be delivered in workable parts.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An incremental approach produces the deliverable through successive additions rather than all at once. Each increment adds usable capability and moves the product closer to completion.

Why it matters and where it applies: it enables earlier delivery of value, earlier stakeholder validation, and lower delivery risk because the team can learn from each addition. It is common in product development, software, digital services, and other work where partial capability is useful before the full solution is finished.

The most common misconception: that incremental and iterative mean the same thing. Incremental delivery is about adding new capability in pieces, while iterative delivery is about revisiting and refining what already exists; many projects use both together, but they are distinct concepts.

How it shows up in real projects: a team releases core reporting first, then adds filters, exports, dashboards, and automated alerts across later releases until the full analytics product is complete.
Contrast: Iterative approach — an incremental approach adds functionality in pieces, while an iterative approach refines the solution through repeated cycles.
Example: A payroll system is delivered first with employee setup, then with time entry, then with tax calculations, and finally with reporting.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Incremental approach, p. 243

Core Meaning: A project life cycle in which the deliverable is produced through a series of increments that each add functionality or capability.
Expansion: An incremental life cycle structures work so the final deliverable emerges in planned pieces, with each increment providing a completed subset of the total scope. PMI describes it as a life cycle suited to situations where value can be delivered progressively and stakeholders can benefit from partial capability before the entire product is complete. It is often combined with iterative practices, but its defining feature is staged addition of usable functionality.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An incremental life cycle divides the solution into multiple releases or build stages, each of which delivers a working portion of the intended outcome. The full scope is achieved by accumulating these completed increments over time.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters when early value, staged deployment, or phased capability reduces risk or improves stakeholder adoption. It commonly applies in software, infrastructure rollouts, product development, and transformation efforts where the whole solution does not need to arrive in one release.

The most common misconception: that incremental always means Agile or that it is the same as iterative. In PMI usage, incremental refers specifically to adding completed capability piece by piece; a team may be incremental with or without strong iteration, and iterative work may occur without releasing distinct increments.

How it shows up in real projects: A team plans release waves, defines which features or components belong in each increment, validates each portion with users, and transitions delivered increments into operations while later increments are still underway.
Contrast: Iterative life cycle — an incremental life cycle delivers the solution in usable pieces; an iterative life cycle refines the solution through repeated cycles.
Example: A hospital records program rolls out patient registration first, clinical documentation second, and billing integration third, with each increment placed into service before the next is completed.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, page 171

Core Meaning: A visible display that shares key project or team information broadly and continuously.
Expansion: An information radiator makes important work information easy to understand at a glance without requiring people to request updates. PMI describes it as a visible, physical display that supports timely knowledge sharing across the organization. It is commonly used in agile settings to improve transparency, align stakeholders, and reduce communication delay.
Contrast: Status report — an information radiator is persistently visible and updated as work changes, while a status report is a periodic communication delivered at set intervals.
Example: A product team maintains a wall board showing sprint goals, work in progress, blockers, and release burnup so stakeholders can see current status immediately.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase, term Information radiator, page 11

Core Meaning: The agile practice of regularly examining work and outcomes and adjusting based on what is learned.
Expansion: Inspect and adapt is a core principle of empirical, agile delivery in which teams frequently review the product and their process and make changes accordingly. It relies on transparency so that inspection reveals real status, and it is enacted through events such as reviews and retrospectives. The practice drives continuous improvement and keeps work aligned to changing needs.
Contrast: Continuous improvement — inspect and adapt is the specific empirical cycle of frequent review and adjustment in agile, while continuous improvement is the broader ongoing pursuit of better processes.
Example: In each sprint retrospective the team inspects its workflow and adapts by adding a code review step to cut defects.
Source: Scrum Guide — empiricism (inspect and adapt)

Core Meaning: A set of characteristics used to judge whether a user story is well formed and ready for adaptive delivery work.
Expansion: INVEST stands for independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, and testable. PMI uses it as a practical check for the quality of user stories in adaptive environments. It helps teams refine backlog items so they are clearer, more deliverable, and easier to estimate and validate.
Contrast: Acceptance criteria — INVEST assesses whether the story itself is well shaped; acceptance criteria define the conditions that prove the story is complete.
Example: A product owner breaks a broad reporting request into smaller stories and checks that each one is valuable, estimable, and testable before sprint planning.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase, INVEST

Core Meaning: A fixed period of repeated work used to create and refine deliverables through progressive development and feedback.
Expansion: An iteration is a short, structured cycle in which a team plans, builds, reviews, and learns from a defined set of work. PMI presents it as a recurring cadence that supports adaptive planning and regular inspection of results. Iterations help teams limit work scope, expose issues early, and incorporate feedback before too much time or cost is committed. They are common in agile and hybrid life cycles, though the idea of repeated refinement also appears more broadly in project work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An iteration is a repeating, usually timeboxed cycle that organizes work into manageable periods. Each cycle aims to produce learning, progress, and often one or more usable increments.

Why it matters and where it applies: iterations reduce uncertainty by shortening the distance between planning, execution, review, and adjustment. They are especially valuable when requirements may evolve, solutions need experimentation, or stakeholders benefit from seeing progress in small steps.

The most common misconception: that iteration means rework caused by poor planning. In disciplined project management, iteration is intentional and structured; it is a planned mechanism for refinement and adaptation, not simply correcting mistakes.

How it shows up in real projects: teams run two-week sprints in software development, repeat design-review-build cycles for a prototype, or refine communications materials across scheduled review rounds until they meet stakeholder needs.
Contrast: Increment — an iteration is the recurring work cycle; an increment is the usable result that emerges from one or more iterations.
Example: A team developing an internal portal works in two-week iterations, selecting priority stories, building and testing them, then reviewing the result with business users before the next cycle begins.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, term Iteration, page 168

Core Meaning: The set of backlog items selected for delivery within a specific iteration, together with the work needed to complete them.
Expansion: An iteration backlog is a short-horizon plan that defines what the team intends to complete during the current iteration or timebox. It is usually drawn from a broader product or project backlog and refined into actionable work based on team capacity, priorities, and acceptance criteria. The backlog may evolve during the iteration as the team learns more, but it remains focused on achieving the iteration goal.
Contrast: Product backlog — the product backlog is the ordered, broader body of candidate work; the iteration backlog is the subset committed or targeted for the current iteration.
Example: At the start of a two-week iteration, a software team selects eight user stories from the product backlog, breaks them into tasks, and tracks that set as the iteration backlog.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A short, shared statement of the single most important outcome a team commits to achieving within an iteration.
Expansion: An iteration goal gives a timebox coherence by naming the value the team intends to deliver, helping members prioritize and make trade-offs when work competes. It aligns the iteration backlog to a purpose rather than a loose list of items, and it provides a clear basis for the review. The goal is negotiated at planning and remains stable through the iteration even if specific tasks change. In Scrum this is expressed as the Sprint goal.
Contrast: Product vision — a product vision describes long-term direction for the whole product, whereas an iteration goal scopes one short timebox.
Example: The team sets an iteration goal to enable guest checkout end to end, then selects backlog items that serve it.
Source: Agile Practice Guide (PMI and Agile Alliance)

Core Meaning: A short-horizon plan that describes the work a team intends to complete during a specific iteration and how it will accomplish it.
Expansion: An iteration plan translates prioritised backlog items or agreed scope into near-term team work for a timeboxed cycle. It typically covers selected work items, capacity, sequencing, and the practical approach for completing the iteration goal. In PMI's agile context, it supports adaptive delivery by aligning the team around what will be done now rather than attempting to detail the whole project upfront.
Contrast: Release plan — an iteration plan focuses on the next short timebox, while a release plan looks across multiple iterations toward a broader delivery target.
Example: At the start of a two-week sprint, a product team selects eight user stories, breaks them into tasks, checks capacity, and commits to a tested dashboard increment by the iteration review.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, page 60

Core Meaning: A planning activity in adaptive delivery used to select and clarify the backlog items the team will complete in the upcoming iteration.
Expansion: Iteration planning is the collaborative session in which the team reviews prioritised work, confirms understanding, and commits to a realistic set of items for the next short cycle. It typically includes clarifying acceptance criteria, discussing effort, and forming the iteration backlog. The practice supports short feedback loops and near-term delivery commitment in adaptive approaches.
Contrast: Iteration review — iteration planning prepares and commits the next cycle's work; iteration review inspects what was completed at the end of the cycle.
Example: Before a two-week sprint starts, the team selects five user stories, refines acceptance criteria, and agrees on what can be delivered by the sprint end.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, p. 52

Core Meaning: A meeting held at the end of an iteration to demonstrate completed work and gather feedback.
Expansion: An iteration review is a structured event for inspecting the results of an iteration with relevant stakeholders. It focuses on what was completed, what value was delivered, and what feedback should influence upcoming work. PMI places it within iterative and agile delivery as a key feedback mechanism. It supports transparency, stakeholder alignment, and informed backlog or priority adjustments.
Contrast: Iteration planning — iteration review examines completed work and feedback at the end of a cycle, while iteration planning selects and prepares the work for the next cycle.
Example: At the end of a two-week cycle, a product team demonstrates the finished onboarding workflow to business users and updates priorities based on their comments.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, iteration review, p. 268

Core Meaning: A development approach in which project work is progressively elaborated and refined through repeated cycles as understanding increases.
Expansion: Iterative work develops a solution through repeated cycles that allow learning from each pass and improving the next one. PMI uses the concept to describe situations where scope may be broadly known but the exact solution emerges through progressive elaboration. It is especially useful when requirements are uncertain, feedback is valuable, or the team needs to refine the product as it learns.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Iterative is a way of developing a deliverable through repeated cycles of planning, building, reviewing, and refining. Each cycle improves understanding of the solution and informs the next pass.

Why it matters and where it applies: it reduces the risk of locking into a poor solution too early and helps teams adapt as knowledge grows. It is common in product development, software, design-heavy work, research-oriented efforts, and other environments where learning changes the answer.

The most common misconception: that iterative simply means incremental. In PMI usage, iterative emphasizes repeated refinement, while incremental emphasizes adding new functionality in pieces.

How it shows up in real projects: a team prototypes a feature, reviews it with users, revises the design, and repeats that loop until the solution meets the need well enough for release.
Contrast: Incremental — iterative focuses on repeated refinement of the solution; incremental focuses on adding functionality in successive usable pieces.
Example: A design team creates an early prototype of a customer portal, tests it with users, updates the navigation and workflow, and repeats several cycles before finalising the release.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition, p. 171

Core Meaning: A development approach that uses repeated cycles to progressively elaborate the product as understanding increases.
Expansion: An iterative approach develops the solution through repeated cycles of work, learning, and refinement rather than fully defining it upfront. PMI describes it as useful when requirements or the solution are not yet fully understood and need progressive elaboration. Each cycle improves the product based on feedback, discovery, or testing. This makes it valuable in environments where learning is part of delivery rather than something completed before execution begins.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An iterative approach is a way of developing a product or deliverable through repeated cycles that progressively refine it. Instead of aiming for a fully finalised solution from the start, the team begins with an early version and improves it as understanding grows.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is useful when requirements are evolving, technical uncertainty is high, or stakeholder feedback can materially improve the outcome. It appears often in software, design, innovation, and complex problem-solving work where learning by doing is more reliable than upfront certainty.

The most common misconception: that iterative means the team is simply redoing work because planning was poor. In disciplined project management, iteration is intentional and structured, with each cycle designed to add clarity, reduce uncertainty, and improve fitness for purpose.

How it shows up in real projects: a team builds a prototype, tests it with users, revises the interface, improves performance, and repeats until the product meets acceptance needs, with each cycle informing the next.
Contrast: Incremental approach — an iterative approach focuses on refining and evolving the solution through repeated cycles, while an incremental approach focuses on adding new functionality in usable pieces.
Example: A team developing a customer portal releases an early dashboard, gathers user feedback, then repeatedly improves navigation, reporting logic, and visual design across later cycles.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, iterative approach, p. 268

Core Meaning: A project life cycle in which the project scope is generally determined early but time and cost estimates are progressively modified as the team's understanding increases through repeated cycles.
Expansion: An iterative life cycle develops the product through repeated cycles that elaborate the solution over time. PMI describes it as suitable when scope is known at a high level but detailed understanding of the solution evolves through successive iterations. Each cycle produces a version or refinement that informs the next, improving estimates, design quality, and stakeholder understanding.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An iterative life cycle is a development approach in which the team revisits and refines the product through repeated cycles instead of attempting to define every detail once at the beginning. The product becomes progressively more complete as learning from each iteration informs the next.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is useful when the goal is understood but the best technical or design solution needs discovery and refinement. It commonly appears in software, product design, engineering development, and other work where feedback improves the solution before full-scale completion.

The most common misconception: that iterative always means incremental or fully Agile. In PMI usage, iterative refers specifically to repeated refinement, while incremental focuses on delivering usable portions, and many projects combine both.

How it shows up in real projects: teams build an early model, prototype, or partial version, review results with stakeholders, adjust requirements or design details, and then repeat the cycle with improved estimates and understanding.
Contrast: Incremental life cycle — iterative focuses on repeated refinement of the same product elements, while incremental focuses on delivering added functionality in usable pieces.
Example: A medical-device team develops a prototype, tests it with clinicians, refines the interface and technical design, and repeats the cycle several times before finalising the product.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, section discussing development approaches and life cycles, including iterative life cycle, page citation requires confirmation in PMI source

Core Meaning: A lean approach that delivers materials exactly when needed in order to minimize inventory and waste.
Expansion: Just-in-time (JIT) manufacturing is a lean production strategy in which materials and components arrive precisely when they are needed in the process, rather than being stockpiled. By minimizing inventory it cuts holding, handling, and obsolescence costs and exposes process problems quickly. It depends on reliable suppliers and stable demand, since there is little buffer to absorb disruption.
Contrast: Pull system - a pull system is the broader principle of producing only in response to demand, of which JIT delivery of materials is a concrete application.
Example: An assembly plant has parts delivered hourly to the line so that almost no warehouse inventory is held.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A scheduling or supply approach that provides materials or information only when they are needed.
Expansion: Just in time reduces excess inventory, waiting, and storage by aligning delivery closely with actual need. In projects, it can apply to materials, components, or information flow when timing precision is important. The retrieved PMI sources do not provide a specific glossary entry or page for this term.
Contrast: Just in case — just in time minimizes early delivery and inventory; just in case keeps extra supply or buffer on hand.
Example: A construction project arranges for specialty fixtures to arrive shortly before installation rather than storing them on site for weeks.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A method for managing and improving work by visualizing workflow, limiting work in progress, and optimizing flow.
Expansion: Kanban makes work visible so teams can manage flow and spot bottlenecks or overcommitment. PMI describes it through visual management and flow-based practices that help teams see work that is planned, in process, or completed. It is especially useful where work arrives continuously and the team wants to improve throughput and predictability.
Deep Dive:
Kanban is a flow-based method that uses visual management to show work status and capacity. A kanban board makes work visible, and the method rests on a few core practices:

- Visualize the workflow so planned, in-progress, and completed work is clear.
- Manage and limit work in progress.
- Improve the flow of work through the system.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps teams identify bottlenecks and overcommitment, which supports better coordination and continuous improvement. It is commonly applied in operational work, product development, and other environments where priorities shift and work is pulled through the system.

The most common misconception is that Kanban is just a board. The board is a tool, but the method also includes managing work in progress and improving workflow.

How it shows up in real projects: a team uses columns for backlog, development, testing, and done, then limits items in progress so blockers become visible sooner.
Contrast: Scrum — Kanban focuses on continuous flow and visualizing work, while Scrum is organized around fixed-length iterations and defined roles and events.
Example: A support team uses a kanban board to track incidents from intake to resolution and limits work in progress so urgent issues do not pile up.
