Source: Agile Practice Guide, Kanban, p. 56

Core Meaning: The point in time at which delaying a decision any further would eliminate an important option or create unnecessary risk.
Expansion: Last Responsible Moment is a lean and agile decision principle that encourages teams to defer irreversible choices until they have enough information to decide well. It does not mean procrastinating; it means preserving flexibility while learning. In project work, it is useful when requirements, technology, or market conditions are still evolving and premature decisions could lock in waste or rework.
Contrast: Progressive elaboration — progressive elaboration is the gradual refinement of plans and details; last responsible moment is the timing principle for when a consequential decision should be made.
Example: A product team delays selecting a cloud-database technology until performance tests on candidate architectures are complete, then commits before integration work would be affected.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The elapsed time between initiating a request, order, or process and having the result available.
Expansion: Lead time is used to understand how long work takes from initiation to delivery or availability. In projects, it commonly applies to procurement, approvals, manufacturing, and supply constraints. It helps teams plan realistically when external or internal waiting periods affect schedule commitments.
Contrast: Lag — lead time is total elapsed time for an item or process; lag is a planned delay between linked schedule activities.
Example: A construction project plans around a six week lead time for switchgear so site installation is not scheduled before delivery is feasible.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A visual measure of how long work items take to move from request or commitment to completion.
Expansion: A lead time chart helps teams inspect delivery speed and predictability by showing the elapsed time for completed work items. It is commonly used in flow-based agile environments to spot delays, variability, and systemic bottlenecks. As a management aid, it supports forecasting and continuous improvement rather than judging individual performance.
Contrast: Burndown chart — a lead time chart shows elapsed completion time per work item, while a burndown chart shows how remaining work changes over time within a timebox or release.
Example: A Kanban team tracks the lead time of service requests and discovers that legal review adds most of the delay, prompting a policy change and clearer intake criteria.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A way of thinking and working that focuses on maximizing value while minimizing waste.
Expansion: Lean emphasizes delivering only what creates value for the customer and removing activities that do not contribute to that value. In PMI usage, it is associated with efficiency, flow, and continuous improvement rather than simply doing more with less. It is especially useful where delays, handoffs, and non-value-added work are reducing performance.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Lean is a management and delivery approach centered on value, flow, and waste reduction. It seeks to improve how work moves through the system rather than optimizing isolated tasks in isolation.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because many project delays come from waiting, rework, excess approvals, and unnecessary handoffs. It applies in product development, operations, construction, and service environments where cycle time and efficiency are important.

The most common misconception: that Lean means cutting cost at all costs. In practice, Lean is about improving value delivery and system performance, not simply reducing resources.

How it shows up in real projects: teams simplify approval steps, reduce batch sizes, remove duplicate reviews, and use visual management to expose bottlenecks.
Contrast: Agile — Lean focuses on flow and waste reduction; Agile focuses on iterative delivery and responsiveness to change.
Example: A project team removes an extra review step that was causing two-week delays without improving quality.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase / Lean

Core Meaning: An approach for developing a new product or business idea by testing assumptions quickly through experiments, customer feedback, and iterative learning.
Expansion: Lean startup applies build-measure-learn thinking to reduce waste and validate whether a proposed solution creates real customer value before large investments are made. In project and product environments, it is used to test hypotheses with minimum viable offerings, gather evidence from users, and pivot or continue based on results. PMI commonly treats it as an adjacent innovation and product-development concept rather than a formal core glossary term.
Contrast: Prototype — a prototype is a sample or model used to explore a solution, while lean startup is the broader evidence-driven approach for testing the business and customer assumptions around a product or venture.
Example: A team exploring a new subscription service launches a minimal pilot to a small customer group, measures adoption and retention, and changes the offering after learning which features users actually value.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A one-page model for capturing and testing assumptions about a proposed product, customer problem, solution, and business viability.
Expansion: Lean Startup Canvas is an innovation and product-discovery tool used to structure hypotheses before significant investment is made. It helps teams clarify customer segments, problems, value proposition, channels, and key measures in a compact format. In project and product environments, it is most relevant during early concept exploration, business-model testing, and uncertain initiative framing.
Contrast: Business case — a Lean Startup Canvas is a lightweight hypothesis and learning tool, while a business case is a formal justification for investment and decision making.
Example: Before funding a new digital service, an internal innovation team uses a Lean Startup Canvas to map the customer problem, define the proposed solution, and identify the riskiest assumptions to test with interviews and prototypes.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A queuing principle stating that average cycle time equals the average work in progress divided by the average throughput.
Expansion: Little's Law states that the average time an item spends in a system, its cycle time, equals the average number of items in progress divided by the average completion rate, or throughput. The practical lesson for agile and Kanban teams is that the more work they take on at once, the longer each item takes to finish. Limiting work in progress is therefore the most direct lever for shortening delivery times.
Contrast: Velocity - velocity measures how much work a team completes per iteration, whereas Little's Law relates work in progress, throughput, and how long each item takes.
Example: A Kanban team halves its work-in-progress limit and sees the average cycle time per story drop accordingly.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A concept used to define the scope of the first release of a solution to customers by identifying the fewest number of features or requirements that would deliver value.
Expansion: Minimum viable product defines the first release of a solution around the smallest set of features that still provides usable value. PMI treats it as a deliberate scope choice for learning and delivery, not simply a stripped-down version of a product. It is especially relevant in adaptive, iterative, and product-oriented environments where early feedback improves decisions.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A minimum viable product is the earliest usable release that delivers real value with the fewest necessary features. It is designed to test assumptions, support learning, and create a basis for further increments.

Why it matters and where it applies: MVP reduces the risk of building too much before validating demand, usability, or fit. It is common in digital product development, innovation work, and adaptive projects where customer feedback can reshape priorities.

The most common misconception: Many people treat MVP as a low-quality or incomplete product. In practice, it must still be viable, meaning useful enough to deliver value and generate meaningful feedback.

How it shows up in real projects: Teams define a narrow first-release scope, launch it to a target user group, measure outcomes, and then reprioritize the backlog based on what they learn.
Contrast: Prototype — an MVP is a usable first release that delivers value; a prototype is mainly built to explore, test, or demonstrate an idea.
Example: A university launches a new student app with course schedules, notifications, and fee payment as the MVP, then adds advising and social features in later releases.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis / Minimum viable product, p. 401

Core Meaning: An agile practice in which the whole team works together on the same item at the same time.
Expansion: Mobbing, or mob programming, has several team members collaborate simultaneously on a single backlog item or problem, typically at one workstation with one person driving and the rest navigating. It speeds knowledge sharing and improves quality on complex work, at the cost of concentrating many people on one item at once.
Contrast: Swarming - swarming has team members converge to finish work that is already partly done, whereas mobbing has the whole team work one item together from the start.
Example: A team tackles a tricky architecture spike by mobbing, with everyone at one screen sharing ideas in real time.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A visual chart where team members record their daily mood to surface morale trends over time.
Expansion: A Niko-Niko calendar, also called a mood board, is an information radiator on which each team member marks a simple happy, neutral, or unhappy face each day. Tracking these moods across a sprint reveals patterns that can prompt the team to address morale or impediment issues in retrospectives.
Contrast: Mood Chart - the two are essentially synonymous, though Niko-Niko specifically refers to the smiley-face daily logging that originated in agile practice.
Example: After two weeks the Niko-Niko calendar shows a dip in morale every Thursday, prompting the team to investigate the recurring late-day deployment crunch.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An agile practice in which two developers work together at one workstation, one writing code while the other reviews in real time.
Expansion: Pair programming, a core Extreme Programming practice, has two developers collaborate on the same task at a single workstation: the driver writes the code while the navigator reviews each line, thinks ahead, and catches issues immediately. The pair switch roles frequently to stay fresh and share understanding. Although two people produce one stream of code, the continuous review tends to improve quality, spread knowledge, and reduce defects and key-person risk.
Contrast: Code review - pair programming reviews code continuously as a co-located pair writes it, whereas a code review inspects completed work asynchronously after it is submitted.
Example: Two developers pair on a tricky payment module so defects are caught as they are written and both fully understand the code afterwards.
Source: Beck - Extreme Programming Explained (pair programming)

Core Meaning: A deliberate change in strategy or direction made in response to validated evidence that the current approach is not working.
Expansion: Originating in lean startup thinking, a pivot is a structured course correction that keeps the underlying vision while changing the product, customer, or business-model hypothesis being tested. It is triggered by learning, not whim, and is distinguished from minor adjustments by the scale of the change. Pivoting early, before heavy investment, limits sunk cost and preserves options. The alternative to pivoting is to persevere on the current path.
Contrast: Scope creep — scope creep is uncontrolled, unplanned expansion of scope, whereas a pivot is an intentional, evidence-based redirection.
Example: After data shows businesses, not consumers, adopt the tool, the team pivots its target market and repositions the product.
Source: Lean Startup (Eric Ries)

Core Meaning: A collaborative estimation technique in which team members use relative estimates and reveal them together to converge on effort or complexity.
Expansion: Planning poker is commonly used in Agile teams to estimate backlog items by discussion and simultaneous reveal, often with story-point values. Its purpose is to reduce anchoring bias, surface differing assumptions, and build shared understanding of the work. PMI discusses it as an Agile estimation practice rather than a formal glossary term.
Contrast: Expert judgment — planning poker is a structured group estimation technique with simultaneous reveal; expert judgment may come from one or more specialists without that facilitation format.
Example: A Scrum team estimates user stories for an upcoming iteration by selecting cards privately, revealing them at the same time, then discussing why one developer chose 3 while another chose 13.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An ordered list of work to be done on a product.
Expansion: A product backlog contains the features, enhancements, fixes, and other work items that may be needed to evolve a product. It is ordered so the team can focus on the most valuable work first and refine items as understanding improves. In adaptive delivery, it is a living artifact that changes as feedback, learning, and priorities change.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A product backlog is the evolving source of work for a product team. It typically includes user stories, defects, technical work, and other items needed to deliver value.

Why it matters and where it applies: it gives teams a single prioritized view of what may be built next. It is central in agile and hybrid environments because it supports continuous reprioritization, refinement, and planning.

The most common misconception: that the backlog is a fixed requirements list. In practice it is intentionally changeable and is refined as the team learns more about customer needs, technical constraints, and business value.

How it shows up in real projects: a product owner reviews feedback from users, reorders backlog items before each iteration, and adds new work discovered during testing or stakeholder review.
Contrast: Requirements documentation — a product backlog is a dynamic ordered work list; requirements documentation is typically a more formal statement of needs and constraints.
Example: A mobile app team keeps a product backlog of login improvements, accessibility fixes, and new payment features, then selects the top items for the next sprint.
Source: Agile Practice Guide / PMI Lexicon — product backlog

Core Meaning: The product goal describes a future state of the product that serves as the long-term objective for the Scrum Team.
Expansion: In PMI's agile and Scrum context, the product goal gives the team a durable target that guides backlog ordering and incremental delivery. It provides coherence across multiple iterations by tying near-term work to an intended product outcome. It is especially useful when teams need a shared direction without fixing all detailed requirements upfront.
Contrast: Sprint goal — a product goal is the longer-term objective for the product, while a sprint goal is the near-term objective for a single sprint.
Example: A digital-banking team sets a product goal to enable customers to open an account fully online, then orders backlog items over several sprints to achieve that outcome.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / The Standard for Project Management — glossary; Scrum Guide reproduced in Agile Practice Guide context, exact page citation requires source verification

Core Meaning: A person responsible for maximizing the value of the product and accountable for the end product.
Expansion: The product owner is the role accountable for maximizing product value by deciding what work should be done first and what should be deferred. In PMI usage, the role is central in adaptive and agile settings where priorities change as teams learn. It connects stakeholder needs and business goals to the team's ordered work. This makes it a key decision point for value-focused delivery.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The product owner is the person who makes prioritization decisions for the product and is accountable for the value of what is produced. The role turns stakeholder needs, feedback, and business objectives into an ordered flow of work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because teams need a clear authority for tradeoff decisions when time, capacity, and demand compete. It applies most directly in agile and adaptive delivery, especially where products evolve through frequent feedback and incremental release.

The most common misconception: that the product owner is only a backlog administrator or stakeholder messenger. In practice the role carries real decision authority over value and priority, not just record keeping.

How it shows up in real projects: the product owner reviews customer feedback, reorders the backlog, clarifies acceptance expectations, and decides that a high-value checkout improvement should be built before lower-impact interface changes.
Contrast: Project manager — the product owner focuses on product value and prioritization; the project manager focuses on coordinating project work and delivery.
Example: On an e-commerce initiative, the product owner prioritizes payment reliability features ahead of cosmetic storefront updates because failed transactions hurt business value more.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, product owner, page 168

Core Meaning: A product roadmap is a high-level visual summary of a product's vision, direction, priorities, and progress over time.
Expansion: In PMI product management usage, a product roadmap communicates how planned work is intended to advance product goals and deliver value over time. It aligns stakeholders around likely sequencing, major outcomes, and strategic intent rather than serving as a fixed delivery schedule. It is commonly updated as learning, market conditions, and priorities change.
Contrast: Release plan — a product roadmap shows strategic direction and higher-level sequencing; a release plan details nearer-term delivery content and timing.
Example: A digital-banking program uses a roadmap to show that onboarding simplification comes before savings-goal features and later expands into small-business services.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A product vision is a concise description of the desired future state a product is intended to create for users and the organization.
Expansion: The product vision expresses the aspirational end state that gives the product direction and purpose. It helps teams and stakeholders align on what they are trying to achieve before debating detailed features or delivery sequencing. In PMI-aligned product and agile practice, it anchors prioritization and supports consistent decision-making as conditions change.
Contrast: Project vision — a product vision describes the enduring future of the product and its value; a project vision is tied to the temporary effort delivering change.
Example: A university defines the product vision for its student portal as one seamless digital front door for enrollment, advising, billing, and academic support.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A lean scheduling technique in which work or materials are produced only in response to actual downstream demand.
Expansion: A pull system limits work in progress by starting new work only when there is a signal of demand, rather than pushing work based on a forecast or schedule. Often visualized with kanban boards and signals, it reduces waste, overproduction, and inventory while exposing bottlenecks. It is a foundational concept in lean and many agile approaches.
Contrast: Push system - a push system produces to forecast and pushes output downstream, while a pull system produces only to actual demand.
Example: A kanban team pulls the next user story into development only when a slot frees up, capping work in progress at three items.
Source: Agile Practice Guide - lean and kanban concepts

Core Meaning: The disciplined improvement of a product's internal structure without changing its external behavior.
Expansion: Refactoring is commonly used in agile and software development environments to improve maintainability, readability, flexibility, or technical quality while preserving the functionality experienced by users. It reduces technical debt and makes future changes easier, safer, and faster. Within PMI-aligned agile practice, refactoring supports sustainable delivery by helping teams keep the evolving solution healthy over time.
Contrast: Defect repair — refactoring improves internal design without changing external behavior, while defect repair corrects behavior that is wrong or failing.
Example: During a product increment, a development team reorganizes duplicated code modules and simplifies interfaces so future features can be added faster without altering what users see in the application.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An estimating approach that sizes work by comparison with other work items rather than by assigning absolute units directly.
Expansion: Relative estimating compares one item with another to judge size, effort, or complexity. It is especially useful when exact detail is limited or when a team wants faster, more consistent sizing across a backlog or work set. PMI commonly associates it with agile ways of working, where the focus is on comparative understanding more than precision early on.
Contrast: Analogous estimating — relative estimating compares work items to each other for size; analogous estimating predicts an effort or duration using data from a previous similar activity or project.
Example: A delivery team sizes new backlog items against a reference story and assigns story points based on comparative complexity.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, Estimate Size section, p. 104

Core Meaning: A sizing technique that estimates work by comparing items to one another rather than predicting absolute duration or effort directly.
Expansion: Relative estimation is used to assess the comparative size, complexity, or effort of backlog items by placing them against reference items or agreed scales such as story points. It is especially common in agile environments where uncertainty makes precise early estimates unreliable. By focusing on comparison rather than exact prediction, teams can estimate faster, reduce false precision, and improve planning consistency over time.
Contrast: Bottom-up estimating — relative estimation compares work items against each other, while bottom-up estimating aggregates detailed estimates of individual components into a total.
Example: A Scrum team judges one user story as roughly twice as complex as a previously completed story and assigns it 8 points instead of estimating exact hours upfront.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A deployable version of a product, service, or result that is made available to users or customers.
Expansion: A release is the point at which completed work is packaged and made available for use, deployment, or transition. In PMI contexts, it often refers to a planned delivery of features or capability rather than every internal increment the team completes. Release decisions consider readiness, quality, stakeholder expectations, and operational timing. The concept is especially common in agile, hybrid, and product delivery environments.
Contrast: Increment — an increment is completed usable work; a release is the act or event of making that work available to users or customers.
Example: A software team completes several tested features across three iterations and then combines them into a quarterly release for customer deployment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A high-level plan for delivering a product, deliverable, or increment into use.
Expansion: A release plan identifies what will be released, when it will be released, and the major conditions that must be met. It helps coordinate development, testing, readiness, and stakeholder communication. PMI uses it to align delivery timing with business and operational needs.
Contrast: Roadmap — a release plan is more detailed and delivery-focused; a roadmap is a higher-level strategic timeline of major milestones and decision points.
Example: A software team plans a beta release in June, a pilot release in August, and full production release after security sign-off.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — release plan

Core Meaning: The activity of determining what functionality or scope will be delivered in a release and when that release is intended to occur.
Expansion: Release planning is commonly used in agile and hybrid delivery to map backlog items or features into a target release. It connects product priorities, capacity, dependencies, and timing so teams can communicate what is likely to be delivered together. PMI discusses release planning in agile practice, but it is generally used as a practitioner term rather than a formal PMI glossary entry.
Contrast: Iteration planning — release planning looks across multiple iterations toward a deployable release; iteration planning details the work for the next short cycle.
Example: A digital-banking team groups authentication improvements, account alerts, and profile updates into a quarter-end mobile-app release based on team capacity and regulatory deadlines.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The servant-leader and chief facilitator of an Agile Release Train in the Scaled Agile Framework.
Expansion: In SAFe, the Release Train Engineer coordinates the Agile Release Train, the set of multiple agile teams working toward a common set of objectives. The RTE facilitates program-level events such as PI planning, manages risks and dependencies across teams, and removes impediments, acting in effect as a chief Scrum Master at scale.
Contrast: Scrum Master - a Scrum Master serves one team, whereas the RTE serves the entire release train of multiple teams.
Example: The RTE facilitates quarterly PI planning so eight teams align their commitments and surface cross-team dependencies.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Two team members working together on the same task from different locations using virtual collaboration tools.
Expansion: Remote pairing replicates the benefits of face-to-face pair programming or pair working when team members are distributed, using shared screens, voice, and video links. It supports knowledge sharing, real-time review, and collective ownership across dispersed agile teams.
Contrast: Co-location - co-location places people physically together, while remote pairing achieves close collaboration virtually.
Example: Two developers in different cities share a screen over video to write and review code together in real time.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The elapsed time an item waits in a queue before active work on it begins.
Expansion: Response time is a kanban and flow metric measuring the delay between when an item is requested or committed and when work actually starts on it. Reducing response time, alongside cycle time, improves a team's overall lead time and responsiveness to demand.
Contrast: Cycle time - response time is the wait before work starts, while cycle time is the active duration once work has begun.
Example: A support ticket sits in the backlog for two days before a developer picks it up, a two-day response time.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A meeting held to review what happened in an iteration or project phase and identify improvements.
Expansion: A retrospective helps the team inspect its process, collaboration, and outcomes so it can improve future performance. It is commonly used in adaptive and iterative environments, but the underlying idea of learning from experience applies broadly. PMI treats it as a structured opportunity for continuous improvement.
Contrast: Lessons learned — a retrospective is a live team reflection used to improve the next cycle; lessons learned are the recorded insights that may be captured for future use.
Example: After a two-week sprint, the team agrees to improve backlog refinement and reduce handoff delays before the next iteration.
Source: PMI Agile Practice Guide — retrospective

Core Meaning: An ordered backlog that reflects risk and uncertainty alongside value when deciding what to address next.
Expansion: A risk-adjusted backlog is a practitioner prioritisation approach in which backlog items are sequenced not only by business value but also by exposure, uncertainty, dependency, or technical risk. It helps teams surface unknowns earlier, test risky assumptions sooner, and reduce the chance that major issues remain hidden until late in delivery. In adaptive work, this can improve learning speed and reduce rework, even when lower-risk items appear more immediately attractive from a pure value perspective.
Contrast: Product backlog — a product backlog is the overall ordered list of work; a risk-adjusted backlog is that list deliberately reordered to address risk earlier or more explicitly.
Example: A product team moves a complex third-party integration story ahead of several user-interface enhancements because the integration carries the highest technical uncertainty.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A framework for applying agile and lean practices across many teams in a large enterprise.
Expansion: The Scaled Agile Framework, or SAFe, provides structured roles, events, and artifacts to coordinate multiple agile teams working toward shared business objectives. It organizes teams into an agile release train that plans and delivers together on a common cadence, and layers portfolio, program, and team concerns to align strategy with execution. SAFe is one of several approaches to scaling agile beyond a single team.
Contrast: Scrum guides one team, while the Scaled Agile Framework coordinates many teams and aligns them to enterprise strategy.
Example: An enterprise launches an agile release train of eight teams that plan together in quarterly increment planning.
Source: Scaled Agile Framework

Core Meaning: A lightweight framework for generating value through adaptive solutions in complex work.
Expansion: Scrum is a specific agile framework built around short iterations, defined accountabilities, and frequent inspection and adaptation. It helps teams work through uncertainty by maintaining a prioritised backlog, delivering increments, and reviewing results regularly. PMI treats Scrum as one of several agile approaches rather than as a synonym for all agile practice.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Scrum is a framework for managing complex work through iterative delivery, transparency, and regular adaptation. It defines a small set of roles, events, and artifacts that help teams organise work around value.

Why it matters and where it applies: Scrum is useful where requirements evolve, feedback is important, and teams benefit from delivering in small increments. It is widely applied in software, digital products, and other environments where learning and reprioritisation are frequent.

The most common misconception: that Scrum is merely a collection of meetings or that it fits every project unchanged. In practice, it is a disciplined framework with clear accountabilities and is most effective when the work genuinely benefits from iterative learning.

How it shows up in real projects: a team works in short sprints, refines a product backlog, reviews increments with stakeholders, and uses retrospectives to improve both delivery and teamwork.
Contrast: Agile — Scrum is a specific framework within agile ways of working; agile is the broader mindset and principles.
Example: A customer portal team uses two-week sprints, backlog refinement, sprint reviews, and retrospectives to release new features in small increments.
Source: Agile Practice Guide — Scrum, page 126

Core Meaning: The Scrum role that enables effective use of Scrum by coaching the team, supporting events and practices, and helping remove impediments.
Expansion: A Scrum Master is an accountability within Scrum focused on process enablement rather than task direction. In project settings, this role helps the team maintain effective collaboration, protects the cadence of Scrum events, and supports continuous improvement. The role is especially relevant in adaptive delivery environments where teams rely on transparency, inspection, and adaptation.
Contrast: Project manager — a Scrum Master coaches the Scrum framework and team effectiveness, while a project manager has broader accountability that may include scope, schedule, cost, and governance integration.
Example: On a product delivery effort, the Scrum Master facilitates sprint reviews and retrospectives, helps the team resolve blockers, and coaches stakeholders on backlog refinement practices.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The self-managing Scrum unit made up of the Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Developers who together deliver value.
Expansion: A Scrum team is the core delivery team structure used in Scrum. It is intentionally small, cross-functional, and accountable for creating a usable increment each sprint. PMI describes it as self-managing, meaning members collaborate to decide how best to accomplish the work within the Scrum framework.
Contrast: Project team — a Scrum team is a specific agile team structure with defined Scrum accountabilities, while a project team is the broader term for people working on a project.
Example: In a product-development effort, a Scrum team with one Product Owner, one Scrum Master, and five Developers delivers a tested feature increment every two weeks.
Source: PMI Agile Practice Guide, Scrum team, page 13

Core Meaning: A cross-functional team in which people assume leadership as needed to achieve the team's objectives.
Expansion: A self-organizing team decides internally how to accomplish its work rather than waiting for detailed direction on every task. Team members coordinate, adapt, and take initiative based on the work at hand and the skills available. PMI describes it as a cross-functional team that fluidly assumes leadership as needed to meet objectives.
Contrast: Self-managed team — self-organizing team emphasizes shared, situational leadership; self-managed team is often used more broadly for teams that manage their own work with less external direction.
Example: A product team assigns testing, design, and coding tasks among themselves during a sprint based on current priorities and expertise.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — self-organizing team

Core Meaning: A product development approach that explores multiple design options in parallel and narrows them over time based on learning and constraints.
Expansion: Set-based design keeps several feasible alternatives open instead of selecting a single solution too early. It supports learning, trade-off analysis, and delayed commitment until enough evidence exists to converge on the best option. In PMI usage, it is most often associated with Lean, product development, and environments with high uncertainty or complex technical dependencies.
Contrast: Point-based design — Set-based design keeps multiple viable options open while point-based design commits early to one option and iterates around it.
Example: An engineering team evaluating a new medical device develops three viable enclosure concepts, tests each against manufacturability and safety constraints, and eliminates options only after evidence shows which design best meets requirements.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An agile engineering principle of building the simplest solution that meets current requirements without speculative complexity.
Expansion: Simple design holds that a system should do everything required today and nothing more, avoiding features and abstractions added in anticipation of needs that may never arise. It is an Extreme Programming practice that keeps code easy to understand and cheap to change, relying on refactoring to evolve the design as real requirements emerge. The aim is to defer complexity until it is genuinely justified.
Contrast: Big design up front specifies the full architecture early, while simple design lets it emerge incrementally.
Example: A developer implements only the rules the current story requires, refactoring later when a new requirement actually appears.
Source: Extreme Programming; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A short, time-boxed effort to research or prototype in order to reduce uncertainty.
Expansion: In agile, a spike is a time-boxed investigation used to answer a technical or functional question, gain knowledge, or reduce risk before committing to an estimate or design. Spikes produce learning or a throwaway prototype rather than shippable product. They are common when a team faces an unfamiliar technology or an ambiguous requirement.
Contrast: User story - a user story delivers customer-facing value, whereas a spike produces information needed to deliver that value.
Example: A team spends two days building a throwaway proof of concept to test whether a new API can handle the expected load.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A timeboxed interval within a project during which a usable and potentially releasable increment of a product is created.
Expansion: A sprint is a fixed-length period used in adaptive delivery to produce a usable increment. It provides a regular cadence for planning, execution, review, and adaptation. PMI describes it as a timeboxed interval that supports iterative delivery and frequent feedback.
Contrast: Iteration — sprint is the specific Scrum timebox for producing an increment; iteration is the broader term for a repeated cycle of work.
Example: A product team runs two-week sprints to build, test, and review a new customer self-service feature.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — sprint

Core Meaning: A list of work items identified by the Scrum team to be completed during the Scrum sprint.
Expansion: The sprint backlog contains the work the team has selected for the current sprint, along with the plan for delivering it. It is used by the Scrum team to organise and track progress during the sprint. PMI describes it as the set of work items identified for completion within the sprint.
Contrast: Product backlog — sprint backlog is the subset of work committed for one sprint; product backlog is the broader ordered list of all desired work for the product.
Example: For a two-week sprint, a team pulls five user stories and several technical tasks into the sprint backlog to deliver a login enhancement.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — sprint backlog

Core Meaning: A single objective for the sprint that gives the Scrum Team a shared purpose for the work selected.
Expansion: The sprint goal states what the team intends to achieve by the end of the sprint and keeps attention on the outcome rather than only on completing tasks. It gives the team flexibility in how to perform the work while preserving alignment on the intended value. In PMI-aligned agile practice, it is a Scrum planning concept used to guide daily decisions throughout the sprint.
Contrast: Product goal — a sprint goal is the short-term objective for one sprint, while a product goal is the longer-term objective for the product.
Example: A team sets a sprint goal to enable customers to reset passwords without contacting support, then selects backlog items that support that outcome.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, Appendix X3, page 168

Core Meaning: Sprint planning is the event in Scrum where the team determines what can be delivered in the sprint and how that work will be accomplished.
Expansion: Sprint planning initiates a sprint by aligning the Scrum Team on a sprint goal, selecting backlog items, and discussing the work needed to create a usable increment. It connects product priorities to near-term delivery commitments and gives the team a shared plan for the upcoming iteration. In PMI context, it is a standard agile planning practice used in adaptive delivery environments rather than a universal project-management process for all projects.
Contrast: Iteration planning — sprint planning is the Scrum-specific planning event for a sprint, while iteration planning is the broader generic term used across adaptive methods.
Example: A software team begins a two-week sprint by selecting high-priority backlog items, defining a sprint goal for customer login improvements, and breaking the work into tasks for design, coding, testing, and review.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A Scrum event held after the sprint to inspect how the team worked and identify improvements for the next sprint.
Expansion: The Sprint Retrospective focuses on the team's process, collaboration, tools, and working practices rather than on the product increment itself. It gives the Scrum Team a recurring chance to reflect on what went well, what did not, and what to change next. In PMI-aligned agile practice, it supports continuous improvement, team learning, and adaptation of the way of working.
Contrast: Sprint Review — the Sprint Retrospective examines the team's way of working, while the Sprint Review examines the product increment with stakeholders.
Example: At the end of a sprint, the team agrees to tighten backlog refinement and reduce handoff delays before the next iteration begins.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, Appendix X3, page 169

Core Meaning: A Scrum event held at the end of a sprint to inspect the increment with stakeholders and adapt the backlog based on feedback.
Expansion: The sprint review is the point in the sprint cycle when the team presents what was completed and discusses the current state of the product with stakeholders. Its purpose is to gather feedback, review progress toward the product goal, and inform what should happen next. In PMI-aligned agile practice, it is an inspect-and-adapt event focused on value and learning, not just a status meeting or formal sign-off session.
Contrast: Sprint Retrospective — the sprint review examines the product increment with stakeholders, while the sprint retrospective examines the team's process and collaboration.
Example: At the end of a two-week sprint, a product team demonstrates a working checkout feature, captures stakeholder feedback on usability, and reprioritises backlog items for the next sprint.
Source: Agile Practice Guide / The Scrum Guide — Sprint Review, exact PMI page citation not confirmed

Core Meaning: A visual arrangement of user stories that shows the flow of user activities and helps plan releases or iterations around slices of value.
Expansion: A story map organises backlog items by the user journey or workflow rather than as a flat priority list. In agile practice, it helps teams see the big picture, identify meaningful increments, and decide what belongs in an initial release versus later iterations. It is especially useful when teams need both customer-context visibility and practical release planning.
Contrast: Product backlog — a story map arranges stories in the context of user activities and flow; a product backlog is the ordered list of work items to be delivered.
Example: A product team maps the customer journey for online booking, then selects the minimum set of stories across search, selection, payment, and confirmation for the first release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A relative unit estimating the effort to implement a backlog item rather than its calendar duration.
Expansion: Story points express the relative size of a user story by combining effort, complexity, and uncertainty, often using a Fibonacci-like scale. Because they are relative and team-specific, they let teams estimate quickly and compute velocity without committing to exact hours. Point values should not be compared across different teams.
Contrast: Ideal Time - ideal time estimates effort in absolute hours or days, whereas story points are relative and unitless.
Example: A team scores a simple login screen at 2 points and a complex reporting feature at 13.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A way of working that enables a team to maintain effective delivery over time without burnout or chronic overextension.
Expansion: In PMI's agile context, sustainable pace means matching work demand to the team's real capacity so delivery remains steady, quality stays acceptable, and people can continue performing effectively. It supports predictable flow, healthier teams, and better long-term value delivery rather than short bursts of output followed by fatigue or rework. The idea is closely associated with agile and adaptive environments where cadence, capacity, and team wellbeing directly affect outcomes.
Contrast: Overtime — sustainable pace is a repeatable delivery rhythm; overtime is a temporary increase in effort that cannot be relied on as a normal operating model.
Example: A product team limits sprint commitments to its observed throughput so it can deliver every iteration without weekend work or rising defect rates.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A collaborative technique in which multiple team members focus together on one high-priority work item to complete it faster and reduce delay.
Expansion: Swarming is commonly used in agile and flow-based environments when finishing a critical item is more valuable than keeping everyone on separate tasks. Instead of maximising individual utilisation, the team temporarily concentrates capacity on shared completion. This helps reduce work in progress, accelerate feedback, and expose blockers quickly.
Contrast: Pair programming — swarming involves several people collaborating on one item more broadly; pair programming is a specific two-person development practice.
Example: When a production defect threatens a release, a developer, tester, business analyst, and UX specialist work together on the same issue until it is resolved and validated.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An agile practice where multiple team members focus together on one work item to finish it.
Expansion: Swarming has the whole team or several members converge on a single backlog item or blocked task to complete it quickly, rather than each working separate items in parallel. It reduces work in progress, accelerates flow, and helps clear impediments. It is common in Scrum and Kanban when finishing started work is more valuable than starting new work.
Contrast: Mobbing - mobbing has the team work together continuously as a default way of working, whereas swarming is the targeted convergence on one item to get it done.
Example: Near the end of a sprint, three developers drop their own tasks to jointly finish the last story needed for the increment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The rate at which a unit must be completed to meet customer demand, calculated as available time divided by demand.
Expansion: Takt time, from Lean manufacturing, sets the pace of production by dividing available working time by customer demand over that period, giving the maximum time allowed per unit to keep up with demand. Used in location-based and Lean scheduling, it synchronizes flow and exposes bottlenecks where work exceeds the takt.
Contrast: Cycle Time - takt time is the demand-driven target pace you must meet, while cycle time is the actual time a unit takes to complete.
Example: With 8 productive hours and demand for 16 units a day, takt time is 30 minutes, so each station must finish a unit every half hour.
Source: Lean manufacturing - takt time

Core Meaning: A visual representation of the progress of planned work that allows everyone to see the status of tasks.
Expansion: A task board makes work visible by showing tasks and their current status in a shared visual format. PMI describes it as a way for everyone to see the status of the tasks, and notes its connection to a kanban board. It is commonly used to support coordination, transparency, and flow in team-based work.
Contrast: Kanban board — a task board is the general visual display of task status, while a kanban board is a specific implementation used to manage flow.
Example: A software team uses columns for To Do, In Progress, Review, and Done so the whole team can see where each task stands.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, task board

Core Meaning: A deliberate arrangement of team types and their interaction patterns to optimize fast, sustainable software delivery.
Expansion: Team topology, from the Team Topologies model, organizes work around a small number of team types, such as stream-aligned, enabling, complicated-subsystem, and platform teams, and defines clear modes of interaction between them. The approach treats team structure and communication paths as a primary design concern, reflecting that system architecture tends to mirror organizational structure. The goal is to reduce cognitive load and dependencies so teams can deliver value independently.
Contrast: An org chart shows reporting lines, while a team topology defines value-flow and interaction patterns between teams.
Example: An organization sets up a platform team to provide self-service infrastructure, lowering the cognitive load on stream-aligned teams.
Source: Team Topologies; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The implied future cost of rework created when a team chooses a limited or expedient technical solution instead of a more robust one.
Expansion: Technical debt accumulates when teams defer code quality, architecture, testing, or design improvements in order to move faster in the short term. PMI recognizes it as a practical concern in iterative and technology-enabled delivery because it can reduce future velocity, increase defects, and raise change costs. Managing it requires visibility, conscious tradeoff decisions, and periodic repayment through refactoring or remediation work.
Contrast: Defect — a defect is a flaw needing correction in a deliverable; technical debt is the broader future burden created by expedient technical choices, whether or not a visible defect exists.
Example: A software team hardcodes business rules to meet a release date, then later spends several sprints redesigning the rules engine so new products can be added without repeated code changes.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, glossary Technical Debt

Core Meaning: A test-first approach that defines requirements as test cases before constructing the solution.
Expansion: Test-driven development begins by writing tests that express the expected behaviour of a solution before the solution itself is built. The team then develops just enough functionality to pass those tests and refactors as needed. This approach improves requirement clarity, supports quality, and creates a growing regression test suite as development progresses.
Contrast: Behavior-driven development — test-driven development centres on writing tests first to drive implementation, while behavior-driven development expresses expected behaviour in a more business-readable form.
Example: A software team writes automated tests for password reset rules before implementing the reset feature.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Test-driven development

Core Meaning: A body of knowledge focused on improving the system-level performance of an organization, end-to-end process, project portfolio, or project.
Expansion: Theory of constraints holds that every system has at least one limiting factor that governs its overall performance. Instead of trying to optimize every activity equally, it directs attention to the current constraint because improving that point usually improves the whole system. In PMI usage, it is closely associated with flow-based thinking and with critical chain applications in project environments.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Theory of constraints is a management approach that identifies the factor that most limits overall system performance and then manages work around that limit. The aim is to improve the throughput of the whole system rather than the local efficiency of isolated parts.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often slow down because of one scarce resource, one approval gate, or one persistent dependency rather than because every task is equally problematic. This makes theory of constraints useful in schedule planning, resource management, portfolio flow, and environments where too much simultaneous work overwhelms a bottleneck.

The most common misconception: that any delay or difficult task is automatically the constraint. In practice, the true constraint is the element that most restricts end-to-end performance at that moment, and it can move once the current bottleneck is relieved.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may identify a constrained testing group, protect it from interruptions, sequence work to keep it productive, and limit work in progress upstream so the queue does not become chaotic.
Contrast: Critical path — theory of constraints focuses on the system bottleneck that limits overall flow, while critical path focuses on the longest path of dependent activities that determines schedule duration.
Example: A release program is repeatedly delayed by a single cybersecurity review team, so the project manager restructures handoffs and limits parallel submissions to keep that team focused on the highest-value items first.
Source: The Standard for Project Management and A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, glossary entry for theory of constraints; confirm exact page in your edition

Core Meaning: A practice where a business representative, developer, and tester jointly review a requirement before development to build shared understanding.
Expansion: The Three Amigos meeting brings together three perspectives - business or product, development, and testing or quality - to examine a user story or its acceptance criteria before work begins. It surfaces ambiguities, edge cases, and differing assumptions early, and is closely associated with behavior-driven development.
Contrast: Backlog refinement - refinement prepares and estimates many backlog items, while the Three Amigos focuses three roles on clarifying a single story's acceptance criteria.
Example: Before coding the payment story, the analyst, developer, and tester hold a Three Amigos session and discover an unhandled currency-conversion case.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The number of items passing through a process.
Expansion: Throughput measures how much work a system completes over a defined period. It is commonly used in flow-based delivery to understand capacity and performance trends.
Contrast: Cycle time — throughput measures how many items are completed in a period, while cycle time measures how long a single item takes to move through the process.
Example: A service desk tracks that it completed 38 approved change requests in one month and uses that throughput trend to forecast near-term delivery capacity.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A visual chart that shows how many work items are completed over a period of time, helping teams understand delivery rate and flow.
Expansion: A throughput chart tracks the number of items passing through a process over time. In PMI-aligned flow and agile contexts, it is used to inspect whether delivery is stable, to spot trends in output, and to support forecasting. It is a practical performance view rather than a formal PMI glossary entry.
Contrast: Cycle time chart — a throughput chart shows how many items are completed over time, while a cycle time chart shows how long individual items take to move through the process.
Example: A Kanban team reviews weekly throughput and sees that completed work drops whenever a specialist approval step is added, prompting a workflow change.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A fixed period of time allocated for an activity, event, or iteration, after which the work stops or is reviewed.
Expansion: A time box limits how long a team spends on a task or cycle so that work stays focused and decisions are made within a known window. It is commonly used in adaptive and iterative approaches to encourage discipline, feedback, and regular inspection. PMI treats timeboxing as a practical planning and control technique rather than a separate formal deliverable.
Contrast: Deadline — a time box limits the duration of the work itself, while a deadline is the latest acceptable completion date.
Example: A product team uses a two-week time box for each iteration and reviews the increment at the end of every cycle.
Source: Agile Practice Guide — Timeboxing, p. 126

Core Meaning: A fixed period of time allocated to an activity or increment of work, with the end date held constant.
Expansion: A timebox constrains work within a set duration so teams focus on completing the most valuable feasible scope by a known deadline. It is common in agile ways of working for iterations, planning events, workshops, and spikes. By fixing time and adjusting scope as needed, it improves predictability, decision speed, and feedback cadence.
Contrast: Milestone — a timebox is a fixed duration for performing work; a milestone is a zero-duration marker that indicates a significant point or event.
Example: A Scrum team runs a two-week sprint and commits only to the user stories it believes can be completed within that fixed window.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, glossary Timebox

Core Meaning: Fixing a short, set duration for a piece of work and stopping when the time is up.
Expansion: Timeboxing allocates a fixed, usually short period to an activity; when the box expires, work stops and results are reviewed regardless of completeness. It limits scope creep, forces prioritization, and creates a predictable cadence, as in sprints and spikes. The deadline is fixed while the amount of work delivered flexes.
Contrast: Spike - a spike is a specific time-boxed investigation, whereas timeboxing is the general practice of fixing a duration for any activity.
Example: Capping a brainstorming session at 30 minutes and moving on when the time runs out.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A shared, rigorously consistent vocabulary used by developers and domain experts to describe a system.
Expansion: Ubiquitous language is a practice from domain-driven design in which the whole team adopts one common set of terms, grounded in the business domain, for conversations, models, and code. By eliminating translation between business and technical wording, it reduces misunderstanding and keeps the software model aligned with real domain concepts. The language is refined continuously as understanding deepens.
Contrast: Technical jargon separates specialists from the business, while ubiquitous language unifies them around shared meaning.
Example: A team agrees that the word policy means exactly one concept across discussions, documentation, and source code.
Source: Domain-Driven Design; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A short, simple description of a need or feature stated from the perspective of a user or customer.
Expansion: A user story expresses desired functionality in language centered on user value rather than technical design. It is commonly used in agile and other adaptive environments to support conversation, prioritization, and incremental delivery. User stories are intentionally lightweight so they can be refined as the team learns more and prepares work for implementation.
Contrast: Requirement — a user story is a concise user-centered expression of need, while a requirement is a fuller statement of what the solution must do or satisfy.
Example: A team writes, As a field technician, I want to upload a site photo from my phone so that I can document asset condition immediately.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, glossary — user story

Core Meaning: Knowledge about customers or solutions that is confirmed through real evidence from experiments rather than opinion or assumption.
Expansion: Validated learning measures progress by what a team has demonstrably proven about value and demand, not by features shipped. Teams state a hypothesis, build the smallest test that can refute it, and use observed behavior or metrics to accept or reject it. This evidence then drives the decision to persevere or pivot. It reframes early uncertainty as a series of answerable questions instead of a fixed plan to execute.
Contrast: Output-based progress — counting delivered features assumes value, whereas validated learning requires evidence that the value is real.
Example: A landing-page test confirms that 8 percent of visitors will pre-order, giving the team validated learning to fund the build.
Source: Lean Startup (Eric Ries)

Core Meaning: A value stream is the sequence of activities needed to create and deliver value to a customer or end user.
Expansion: In PMI and lean-agile usage, a value stream looks across the full flow of work from request to realized outcome rather than isolating a single team or phase. It helps organizations see how ideas move through development, approval, delivery, and support, including delays and handoffs that reduce value. This perspective is especially useful when work crosses functions, products, suppliers, or operational boundaries.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A value stream is the end-to-end flow through which value is conceived, created, delivered, and often sustained. It spans multiple activities and may cut across projects, products, programs, and operations.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because customers experience outcomes across the whole flow, not inside organizational silos. Teams use value streams in lean, agile, product, and portfolio contexts to improve throughput, reduce delays, and align investments to outcomes.

The most common misconception: that a value stream is just a process map or a single workflow inside one department. In practice it is broader, centered on customer value, and often crosses many teams and systems.

How it shows up in real projects: An organization maps the path from customer request through prioritization, development, testing, release, and support, then redesigns approvals and handoffs to reduce lead time and improve delivery consistency.
Contrast: Process — a process is a defined set of activities for performing work, while a value stream is the broader end-to-end flow of activities that creates customer value.
Example: A bank examines the full path from a customer's online loan application to approval, funding, and account setup, identifying delays between underwriting, compliance review, and system activation.
Source: PMI Lexicon / Agile Practice Guide / PMBOK Guide — Seventh Edition, value delivery and flow discussion; exact page citation requires source verification

Core Meaning: Value stream analysis is the examination of an end-to-end value stream to identify delays, waste, bottlenecks, and opportunities to improve value delivery.
Expansion: This practice studies how work actually flows from demand to delivered outcome and where that flow breaks down. It is used to reveal non-value-adding steps, excessive wait times, rework loops, and coordination gaps across teams or functions. In project and product environments, it supports improvement decisions about governance, staffing, sequencing, automation, and handoffs.
Contrast: Value stream mapping — value stream analysis is the broader evaluation of flow and performance, while value stream mapping is a common technique used to visualize that flow.
Example: A digital-services organization reviews the path from approved feature request to production release and finds that security review queues, not coding time, are the main cause of delayed delivery.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A variation of process flow used to locate delays, queues, or handoffs in a current process.
Expansion: PMI defines a value stream map as a variation of process flows that can be used to locate delays, queues, or handoffs occurring in current processes. It helps teams see where work waits, where it is transferred, and where inefficiencies reduce flow. The technique is used to improve end-to-end delivery by making non-value-adding time visible.
Contrast: Process flow diagram — a value stream map is specifically used to expose delays, queues, and handoffs, while a process flow diagram more generally shows the sequence of steps.
Example: A service team maps its incident-resolution process and finds that most delay occurs while tickets wait for specialist review.
Source: The PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — value stream map, page 107

Core Meaning: A visual technique used to analyse the flow of materials and information needed to deliver value so waste and improvement opportunities can be identified.
Expansion: Value Stream Mapping helps teams see how work moves from request to delivery and where delays, handoffs, rework, or other inefficiencies occur. In PMI usage, it supports value stream analysis and process improvement by making the current flow visible. It is especially useful before redesigning a process or improving delivery performance.
Contrast: Process map — Value Stream Mapping focuses on end-to-end value delivery flow and waste, while a process map may simply document steps.
Example: A product team maps the path from customer request through analysis, development, testing, and release to find approval bottlenecks that slow delivery.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, page 211

Core Meaning: A measure that appears to show performance but does not meaningfully support decisions or action.
Expansion: A vanity metric can look impressive while saying little about whether a project is delivering useful outcomes. Teams should distinguish it from measures that reveal progress, risk, flow, quality, or realised value. In practice, it often distracts attention from decision-relevant indicators.
Contrast: Work performance information — a vanity metric creates the appearance of insight without guiding action; work performance information is analysed data that supports decisions.
Example: A digital product team reports total app downloads as its headline success metric even though retention and active usage are falling.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A measure of expected team productivity, typically expressed as the total story points expected to be delivered during an iteration or sprint.
Expansion: Velocity is used in agile contexts to help teams forecast how much work they can likely complete in future iterations. It is based on the amount of work actually delivered in prior iterations and is most useful for a given team over time rather than for comparing different teams. PMI treats it as a planning aid, not a performance target. It supports release forecasting, iteration planning, and capacity discussions.
Contrast: Throughput — velocity is usually expressed in story points for a team over an iteration; throughput is the count of items completed over a period.
Example: After several sprints, a team averages 30 story points per iteration and uses that velocity to plan the next release.
Source: Agile Practice Guide Glossary — Velocity

Core Meaning: A chart that shows how much work a team completes in each iteration so future delivery can be forecast more realistically.
Expansion: A Velocity Chart displays completed work across successive iterations to help a team understand its delivery pace. PMI discusses velocity in agile planning and forecasting, but the chart itself is better treated as a practitioner expression of that concept than as a formal glossary term. It is most useful when iteration lengths and the team's method of sizing work are reasonably stable.
Contrast: Burndown chart — a Velocity Chart shows completed work per iteration, while a burndown chart shows remaining work over time.
Example: A Scrum team reviews six sprints of completed story points on a Velocity Chart to estimate how much scope it can likely finish in the next release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A way of making work status, flow, constraints, and priorities visible so teams can coordinate and act quickly.
Expansion: Visual management uses visible signals such as boards, task cards, workflow columns, metrics displays, or status indicators to help people understand the current state of work. It improves transparency and supports faster conversations about bottlenecks, dependencies, and priorities. It is commonly used in agile, lean, operations, and hybrid project environments.
Contrast: Information radiator — visual management is the broader practice of making work visible; an information radiator is a specific visible display used to share project information.
Example: A delivery team uses a wall board with columns for ready, in progress, blocked, and done so everyone can see where work is piling up and rebalance tasks.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A period of inactivity in which work cannot proceed because a required input, decision, resource, or condition is not yet available.
Expansion: Waiting occurs when a task pauses because something needed to continue has not arrived or been resolved. It is a common source of delay and flow inefficiency in projects and service work.
Contrast: Blocked — waiting is passive delay due to missing input or timing, while blocked usually implies an obstacle that must be actively removed.
Example: A testing team waits for a corrected build before it can continue execution.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Any use of time, effort, or resources that does not create value in producing the intended product, service, or result.
Expansion: Waste refers to non-value-adding work that consumes resources without improving the outcome. In project environments, it often appears as rework, waiting, unnecessary handoffs, excess approvals, or overprocessing. Managing waste helps improve flow, reduce delays, and focus effort on value delivery.
Contrast: Value-added work — waste consumes resources without contributing value, while value-added work directly helps create the intended outcome.
Example: A project team removes duplicate status-report preparation that took hours each week but was not used for decisions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The systematic elimination of activities and resources that consume effort without adding value for the customer.
Expansion: Waste reduction is the core aim of Lean thinking, targeting any activity that absorbs resources but creates no value, such as waiting, rework, overproduction, excess inventory, and unnecessary motion. By identifying and removing these wastes, teams shorten lead time, lower cost, and improve flow. The discipline relies on continuous observation of the value stream and incremental improvement.
Contrast: Cost cutting may remove value-adding capacity, while waste reduction removes only non-value-adding activity.
Example: A team eliminates a redundant approval queue, cutting cycle time without reducing quality.
Source: Lean; PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: An agile prioritisation method that sequences work by dividing each item's cost of delay by its size or duration.
Expansion: Weighted Shortest Job First, popularised by the Scaled Agile Framework, prioritises a backlog by calculating cost of delay divided by job size or duration, then doing the highest-scoring items first. Cost of delay typically combines user and business value, time criticality, and risk reduction or opportunity enablement. Because it favours high-value, short jobs, WSJF maximises the economic benefit delivered per unit of effort when capacity is constrained.
Contrast: MoSCoW prioritisation - WSJF produces an economic ranking from cost of delay over size, whereas MoSCoW sorts items into must, should, could, and won't have categories without a numeric ratio.
Example: A product owner scores backlog epics by cost of delay over effort and schedules a small, time-critical, high-value feature ahead of a large one.
Source: Reinertsen / SAFe - Weighted Shortest Job First

Core Meaning: A constraint on the amount of work allowed to be in progress at a given time so flow can be managed and bottlenecks exposed.
Expansion: A WIP limit, or work-in-progress limit, caps how many items a person, team, or workflow stage may handle simultaneously. In Kanban-based systems, it helps reduce multitasking, reveal congestion, and improve throughput by encouraging teams to finish work before starting more. PMI treats it as a practical flow-management mechanism used in adaptive delivery environments.
Contrast: Batch size — a WIP limit restricts how much work is active at once, while batch size describes how much work is grouped together for processing or delivery.
Example: A software team sets a WIP limit of three items in the testing column so developers stop starting new features and help clear testing bottlenecks.
Source: Agile Practice Guide / Kanban-focused guidance on limiting work in progress — confirm exact page in PMI source

Core Meaning: Work that has started but has not yet been completed.
Expansion: Work in progress refers to items currently being worked on within a workflow or delivery system. In project and agile practice, it is often tracked to understand flow, expose bottlenecks, and prevent overloading the team with too many simultaneous tasks. Limiting work in progress helps improve throughput, focus, and predictability.
Contrast: Backlog — Work in progress has already been started; backlog items are approved or candidate work not yet in active execution.
Example: A Kanban team limits itself to five active tasks at a time so developers finish current stories before pulling additional work into execution.
Source: PMI Lexicon / Agile Practice Guide — confirm exact citation in Infinity

Core Meaning: A limit on the number of items allowed to be in progress at one time.
Expansion: Work in progress limits help control flow by preventing too much work from starting before existing work is finished. They are used to reduce multitasking, expose bottlenecks, and improve throughput. PMI references them in adaptive and flow-based contexts as a way to manage capacity and maintain focus.
Contrast: Work in progress — work in progress is the work currently underway; work in progress limits are the cap placed on how much can be underway at once.
Example: A software team sets a limit of three items in testing so the team finishes existing defects before starting more test cases.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, glossary, work in progress limits, page 417

Core Meaning: The sequence of steps, handoffs, and decisions through which work moves from initiation to completion.
Expansion: Workflow describes how work progresses across people, roles, or systems. In project settings, it helps clarify how items are processed, reviewed, approved, and completed. It is commonly used to improve visibility, consistency, and efficiency, especially where recurring work or handoffs create delay or confusion.
Contrast: WBS — workflow shows how work moves through steps and handoffs, while a WBS shows the hierarchical decomposition of project scope.
Example: A change-control workflow routes a request through submission, impact review, approval, implementation, and closure.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A set of explicit team norms that defines how project participants will work together day to day.
Expansion: A working agreement sets expectations for collaboration, communication, decision making, meeting behaviour, and conflict handling within a project team. It is usually created by the team rather than imposed externally, and it can be revised as the team learns what improves performance. In PMI-aligned practice, it is closely related to ground rules and team norms that support effective team development and delivery.
Contrast: Ground rules — a working agreement is the team's broader, living set of collaboration norms; ground rules are the specific behavioural rules within it.
Example: At project kickoff, a cross-functional team agrees to raise blockers within 24 hours, keep meetings to agenda and timebox, and resolve design disputes through a designated decision owner.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An Extreme Programming practice of using a shared, simple story or analogy to describe how a system works.
Expansion: The metaphor in Extreme Programming gives the team and stakeholders a common, plain-language picture of the system's architecture and behavior, guiding naming and design decisions. It substitutes a vivid shared mental model for heavy upfront design documentation. A good metaphor keeps everyone's understanding aligned as the system evolves.
Contrast: Architecture document - a formal architecture document specifies structure in detail, whereas an XP metaphor conveys a shared conceptual picture informally.
Example: Describing an e-commerce system as a 'shopping cart and checkout lane' so everyone shares the same mental model.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A high-level plan that outlines expected releases over a year, including timing and major delivery objectives.
Expansion: A yearly release plan is a long-horizon version of release planning used to sequence expected deliveries across the year. It helps align product, delivery, business, and support expectations without locking every detail too early. Teams typically revise it as priorities, capacity, and market conditions change.
Contrast: Release plan — a yearly release plan is the longer-horizon version of a release plan, while release planning is the activity of creating and updating it.
Example: A digital product team maps four planned quarterly releases so engineering, training, and customer support can prepare in advance.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry
